Professor View 01 / 27
Travel English -- 48 Aulas

Lucimara Miyuki Ito

Conversação para viagens, listening de verdade e reuniões internacionais — rumo a Nova York, setembro de 2026

B1 (Intermediário) São Paulo, SP — Perdizes Diretora — Indústria Química 60 min · Online
Progresso Geral0%
Velocidade:
Nome

Lucimara Miyuki Ito

Idade

55 anos

Cidade

São Paulo, SP — Perdizes (40 min de carro até Guarulhos)

Profissão

Diretora de indústria química (Guarulhos) — produção e assuntos regulatórios (Anvisa); reuniões com fornecedores chineses

Nível

B1 (Intermediário) — exposição real forte; o gargalo é a COMPREENSÃO ORAL

Foco

55% Viagens e cotidiano · 20% Listening intensivo · 15% Business pontual e atualidades · 10% Gramática e escrita

Frequência

2x por semana — segundas 18h e quartas 11h, Online (Zoom), 60 min

Total de Aulas

48 aulas (60 min) — meta: Nova York, setembro de 2026

Personagem-Jornada

De: Viajante frequente que “fica patinando” — trava no listening, perdeu a disciplina com a professora que virou amiga e sente que o inglês não evolui apesar das viagens. Nas palavras dela: “Eu tenho um pouco de pressa, porque eu tô meio que, sabe, quando você fica patinando na coisa.”

Para: Diretora que circula por Nova York, conduz reuniões com parceiros chineses e navega feiras internacionais com autonomia real — e que ENTENDE o que o outro está dizendo, na velocidade em que ele diz.

Promessa Transformadora

Lucimara para de “patinar” e passa a entender o que os americanos falam — de Nova York à China, sem depender de ninguém. O gargalo dela NÃO é vocabulário nem coragem: é o canal auditivo. Ela mesma nomeou: “O entendimento. Eu tenho bastante dificuldade. O listening.” Por isso TODA aula deste programa carrega listening de verdade — áudio ElevenLabs, player com controle de velocidade, e um podcast recomendado para os 80 minutos diários de carro. O ouvido se treina como o corpo: ela já malha 5x por semana, então sabe exatamente do que estamos falando.

Forças
  • Exposição real e frequente — viaja ao exterior a cada 2-3 meses (Nova York, Fort Lauderdale, China)
  • Clareza total de objetivo: conversação para viagens. Zero interesse em certificação
  • Autoconhecimento — identificou sozinha o problema da professora-amiga e buscou estrutura
  • Já reservou a janela de estudo: 40 min de ida + 40 de volta, todo dia, ouvindo no carro
  • Motivação intrínseca — o inglês está atrelado ao que ela ama (viajar), não a obrigação
  • Disciplina comprovada em outra área: corre no Ibirapuera e treina 5x por semana, de manhã cedo
Pontos de Melhoria
  • Listening em ritmo nativo — o gap nº 1, declarado por ela mesma
  • Disciplina de estudo autônomo — histórico de irregularidade sem estrutura clara
  • Possível superestimação do nível (autodeclara “intermediário para avançado”; o listening diz B1)
  • Escrita e leitura menos desenvolvidas que a fala — ela pediu explicitamente para trabalhar as duas
  • Crença limitante sobre a idade: “pessoas de mais de cinquenta anos já têm mais dificuldade”. O material desmente isso pela PRÁTICA (progresso visível, baseline gravado) — nunca pelo discurso

Metodologia

1. Listening em TODA aula — o gap nº 1

Dois áudios longos por aula, com player completo e controle de velocidade (0.5x a 1.25x). Protocolo fixo: som primeiro, sem texto; “what did you catch?”; segunda escuta mais lenta; só então as perguntas. Nunca tradução.

2. Os 80 minutos de carro viram sala de aula

Ela mesma ofereceu a janela: “mesmo que eu vá ouvindo no carro, são quarenta minutos de ida, quarenta de volta”. Os Complementares priorizam podcast e áudio (ela assiste pouca série) e sempre com tarefa concreta: 3 frases entendidas, 2 não captadas.

3. Contexto de viagem, não de curso

Aeroporto, hotel, táxi, restaurante, feira internacional, jantar com parceiro chinês. O conteúdo é o que ela vai viver em setembro em Nova York — nunca inglês genérico. Business entra pontual: reuniões com chineses e atualidades.

4. Ritmo calmo, adulto-adulto (REGRA 14 — 50+)

Repetição generosa, pausas, tempo para pensar. Zero gamificação infantil e zero condescendência: ela é diretora de uma indústria. A vivência internacional dela é recurso pedagógico, não enfeite.

5. Vínculo profissional, NUNCA amizade

O ponto crítico do histórico dela: a professora anterior virou amiga e a aula virou conversa. Seja caloroso e mantenha a autoridade pedagógica. Cada aula precisa mostrar PROGRESSO TANGÍVEL — é o que sustenta a motivação dela.

Mapa de Personalidade

IntrovertidaExtrovertida
LúdicaSéria, orientada a resultado
ImpulsivaReflexiva
Aprendiz leitura/escritaAprendiz auditiva/oral
Tolerância ao erro baixaTolerância ao erro alta
Insegura em inglêsConfiante em inglês
Foco profissionalFoco viagem e vida
Lesson 01 -- Pre-class

Who Is Lucimara? -- Diagnostic Session and Personal Introduction

Introduce yourself and talk about your work at an international trade fair in New York. Key words: industry, supplier, regulatory, trade fair, headquarters, negotiate, commute, fluent, confident, stuck, catch, abroad. Structures: present simple (your routine) vs present continuous (right now) vs present perfect (for / since). And the phrases that save any conversation when you do not understand what the other person said.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Take your time — listen twice, then say it out loud. These are the twelve words that describe your work and your routine.

Industry -- a group of companies that make the same kind of product
"I work in the chemical industry."
Supplier -- a company that sells you the materials you need
"Most of our suppliers are in China."
Regulatory -- connected to the official rules a product must follow
"I am responsible for regulatory affairs."
Trade fair -- a big event where companies show their products to buyers
"I go to a trade fair in Lyon every November."
Headquarters -- the main office of a company, where the leadership sits
"Our headquarters is in Guarulhos."
To negotiate -- to talk with someone until you both agree on a price or a deal
"I negotiate prices with our Chinese partners."
Commute -- the trip you make between home and work
"My commute takes forty minutes each way."
Fluent -- able to speak a language easily, without stopping to think
"I want to be fluent before my next trip."
Confident -- sure that you can do something well
"I feel confident when I read, but not when I listen."
Stuck -- not able to move forward, because something is blocking you
"I get stuck when people speak very fast."
To catch -- to hear and understand what somebody said
"Sorry, I didn't catch that."
Abroad -- in or to another country
"I travel abroad every two or three months."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with the correct definition.

Industry
Supplier
Regulatory
Trade fair
Headquarters
To negotiate
Commute
Fluent
Confident
Stuck
To catch
Abroad

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text slowly, twice, and then answer the questions.

Lucimara is a director in the chemical industry. She works in Guarulhos, where the company headquarters is, and she lives in Perdizes, so her commute takes forty minutes each way. Every month she negotiates prices with suppliers in China, and twice a year she goes to a trade fair abroad. "I have worked in this industry for twenty years," she says, "and I have been a director since 2015." Right now, her team is preparing a new product registration, and she is studying English again, because in September she is going to New York. She reads regulatory documents in English without a problem and she feels confident on the phone with old partners. But when an American speaks fast, she gets stuck: she does not catch the end of the sentence. She wants to be fluent — and, more than that, she wants to understand.

1. Why do we say "I have worked in this industry for twenty years" and not "I work in this industry for twenty years"?
A Because it started in the past and it CONTINUES today — present perfect with for.
B Because it is finished and it has no connection with the present.
C Because it is a routine that she repeats every week.
2. "Right now, her team is preparing a new product registration." Why present continuous?
A Because it happens every day — it is her routine.
B Because it is happening NOW, in this period — it is not the usual routine.
C Because it started twenty years ago and it continues.
3. "She has been a director since 2015." What does this sentence tell you?
A She was a director in 2015 and then she left the position.
B She became a director in 2015 and she is STILL a director today.
C She is going to become a director in 2015.
4. Which sentence about her routine is correct?
A "She is negotiating with suppliers every month."
B "She negotiates with suppliers every month, and her commute takes forty minutes."
C "She has negotiated with suppliers every month since twenty years."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Present Simple vs Present Continuous vs Present Perfect (for / since)

GRAMMAR

The three structures behind any personal introduction in English.

FormUseExample
Present Simple
I / you / we + verb (he/she + s)		Your routine, your job title, what is always true.I work in the chemical industry.
Present Continuous
am / is / are + verb-ing		What is happening NOW, this month — not the routine.Right now, I am preparing a registration.
Present Perfect
have / has + past participle		It started in the past and it CONTINUES today. Duration up to now (for / since).I have worked here for twenty years.
Negativehave / has + not + past participle. In speech, contracted: haven't.I haven't been to Lyon this year.
QuestionHow long + have + subject + past participle?How long have you been in the industry?
for vs sincefor + a period of time (for twenty years) · since + a starting point (since 2015)

Watch out -- the classic mistake: in your first language, duration up to now uses the PRESENT tense, so "I work here for twenty years" feels right. In English it is wrong: duration up to now never uses the present simple. The correct form is "I have worked here for twenty years" (in speech, I've worked). And remember: responsible FOR, never "responsible of".

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence — as many times as you want.

"I in the chemical industry for twenty years."
"Right now, I a new product registration."
"Every month, I prices with our suppliers in China."
"I have been in this industry twenty years."
"I have been a director 2015."
"Sorry, I didn't that. Could you say it again more slowly?"

Stage 2: Put the Conversation in Order

Order

You have just met someone at an international trade fair. Put the steps of the conversation in the natural order.

?Say what you do: your job, your industry and what you are responsible for.
?Agree on a next step: exchange cards and set a time to meet.
?Say hello and introduce yourself: your name and your company.
?Ask HIM a question: what does his company do, and what is he looking for?
?Say how long you have been in the industry, with for or since.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to the sentence, repeat it out loud, and only then record. You can listen and record again as many times as you want — repetition is the method, not a sign of difficulty.

I'm a director in the chemical industry, and I'm responsible for production.
I've worked in this industry for twenty years.
Right now, I'm preparing a new product registration.
Sorry, I didn't catch that. Could you say it again more slowly?
I travel abroad every two or three months, mostly to New York.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each real moment of a business trip or an international trade fair.

At a trade fair in New York, someone asks you: "So, what do you do?" You answer:
A "I do the chemical things in a company."
B "I'm a director in the chemical industry, and I'm responsible for production and regulatory affairs."
C "I am responsible of the production since twenty years."
He asks: "How long have you been in the industry?" The best answer is:
A "I am in this industry for twenty years."
B "I've worked in this industry for twenty years."
C "I work in this industry since twenty years."
You want to talk about the project your team is running THIS month. Which sentence is correct?
A "Right now, we're preparing a new product registration."
B "Right now, we prepare a new product registration."
C "Right now, we have prepared a new product registration."
The American speaks fast and you miss the end of the sentence. The most professional move is:
A Smile, nod, and hope that nobody notices.
B "Sorry, I didn't catch that. Could you say it again more slowly?"
C Apologize for your English and change the subject.
You want to confirm that you understood the agreement. You say:
A "Yes, yes, ok, ok."
B "Let me check I understood. You need the bio by Friday, right?"
C "I don't know, sorry."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer — speak for 90 seconds, with no script. This recording is your BASELINE: we will listen to it again in lesson 24 and in lesson 48, and you will hear the difference with your own ears.

You are at an international trade fair in New York and someone you have never met says hello. Introduce yourself: your name, your job in the chemical industry (present simple), what you are responsible for, how long you have been in the industry (present perfect, for / since), and what your team is working on this month (present continuous). Finish by saying how often you travel abroad. Take your time and don't read from a script.

Survival Card -- Lesson 1

1I'm a director in the chemical industry, and I'm responsible for production.
2I've worked in this industry for twenty years.
3Right now, I'm preparing a new product registration.
4Sorry, I didn't catch that. Could you say it again more slowly?
5I travel abroad every two or three months, mostly to New York.
Lesson 02 -- Pre-class

Arriving and Settling In -- Airports, Hotels, and the First Hours in a New City

Landing in New York, going through immigration and checking in at the hotel — the first hours, the ones nobody teaches you. Key words: terminal, carry-on, baggage claim, check-in counter, boarding pass, layover, customs, front desk, concierge, itinerary, confirmation number, jet lag. Structure: present perfect with just / already / yet ("I’ve just landed", "I haven’t checked in yet") and the contrast with the past simple of the story ("I landed at nine and took a cab").
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Take your time — listen twice, then say it out loud. These are the twelve words of the first hours in any new city.

Terminal -- the part of the airport where you get on or off your plane
"My flight to New York leaves from Terminal 3."
Carry-on -- the small bag you take with you inside the plane
"I only travel with a carry-on when the trip is short."
Baggage claim -- the area where you pick up your suitcases after a flight
"Could you tell me where the baggage claim is?"
Check-in counter -- the desk where you show your ticket and leave your bags
"The check-in counter closes one hour before the flight."
Boarding pass -- the document that lets you get on the plane
"I've already downloaded my boarding pass."
Layover -- a short stop in another city between two flights
"I have a two-hour layover in Atlanta."
Customs -- the place where officers check what you are bringing into the country
"I haven't gone through customs yet."
Front desk -- the reception of a hotel, where you check in
"Please leave your bags at the front desk."
Concierge -- the hotel employee who helps guests with restaurants, taxis and tickets
"The concierge has already booked a table for us."
Itinerary -- the plan of your trip, with the dates, the flights and the hotels
"My itinerary says the fair starts at nine."
Confirmation number -- the code that proves you have a reservation
"I have my confirmation number here."
Jet lag -- the tired feeling after a long flight between time zones
"The jet lag always hits me at four in the morning."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with the definition that explains it.

Terminal
Carry-on
Baggage claim
Check-in counter
Boarding pass
Layover
Customs
Front desk
Concierge
Itinerary
Confirmation number
Jet lag

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text slowly, twice, and then answer the questions.

It is nine fifteen at night at Terminal Four. Lucimara has just landed in New York. She landed at nine, walked to passport control and answered two questions: "Business or pleasure?" and "How long are you staying?" — that part is over, and the past simple tells the story. Now she is at baggage claim. Her suitcase has already arrived on belt six; her carry-on has never left her shoulder. She has not gone through customs yet, but she has nothing to declare. At the hotel, the front desk only needs her confirmation number, because she has already completed the online check-in. One problem: the room is not ready yet. So she leaves her bags, and the concierge recommends a small restaurant two blocks away. At eleven she is finally in her room. She has not unpacked yet — and she will not tonight. The jet lag is going to wake her at four in the morning, and her itinerary says the trade fair starts at nine.

1. "She has just landed." What does just add to the sentence?
A It happened a few minutes ago — very recently.
B It happened a long time ago and it is over.
C It is going to happen in a few minutes.
2. "She has not gone through customs yet." What does this sentence say?
A She has already gone through customs.
B She has not gone through customs so far — but she still will. yet = up to now, not.
C She will never go through customs.
3. Why is "She landed at nine, walked to passport control and answered two questions" in the past simple, and not in the present perfect?
A Because the action is still happening now.
B Because it is a SEQUENCE of finished actions, with the time said out loud (at nine) — it is a story.
C Because the result now matters more than the moment.
4. Which sentence is correct at the front desk?
A "I have completed the online check-in yesterday."
B "I've already completed the online check-in, and I have my confirmation number here."
C "I already complete the online check-in yet."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Present Perfect with just / already / yet (and the contrast with the Past Simple)

GRAMMAR

Three small words that change everything.

WordUseExample
just
have + just + past participle		It happened a few minutes ago. It goes BEFORE the past participle.I have just landed. (I’ve just landed.)
already
have + already + past participle		It is done — often earlier than expected. Affirmative sentences. It goes BEFORE the past participle.I have already completed the online check-in.
yet
haven’t + past participle + yet		Not up to now (negative) or "so far?" (question). It always goes AT THE END of the sentence.I haven’t checked in yet. / Have you checked in yet?
Negativehave / has + not + past participle. In speech: haven’t / hasn’t.My bag hasn’t arrived yet.
QuestionHave + subject + past participle ... yet?Have you picked up your bags yet?
Past Simple
verb in the past		When you SAY THE TIME or tell the sequence (a story), English needs the past simple: "I landed at nine and took a cab." NEVER "I have landed at nine."

Watch out -- the two classic mistakes: (1) yet goes AT THE END: "I haven’t checked in yet", never "I haven’t yet checked in" in everyday speech. (2) With an explicit time or date, the present perfect is FORBIDDEN: "I landed at nine" (right) and not "I have landed at nine" (wrong). The practical rule: when WHEN matters → past simple; when THE RESULT NOW matters → present perfect.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence. Tap Listen to hear the whole sentence — as many times as you want.

"I have landed in New York."
"I have completed the online check-in."
"I haven't checked in ."
"My bag arrived yet."
"My flight at nine, and I took a cab to the hotel."
"Here is my ."

Stage 2: Put the Arrival in Order

Order

You have just landed in New York. Put the steps in the real order — from the runway to the hotel front desk.

?Take a taxi to the hotel and give the driver the address.
?Pick up your suitcase at baggage claim, belt six.
?At the front desk, give your name and your confirmation number.
?Land at the terminal and answer the officer at passport control.
?Go through customs -- green line if you have nothing to declare.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to the sentence, say it out loud, and only then record. Pay attention to the linking: I’ve just sounds almost like one single word, and haven’t almost disappears in fast speech — that is exactly what swallows the sentence at the airport.

Excuse me, I have a reservation under the name Ito.
I've just landed, and I haven't checked in yet.
I've already completed the online check-in. Here is my confirmation number.
Could you tell me where the baggage claim is?
My flight landed at nine, and I took a cab straight to the hotel.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each real moment of the first hours in New York.

You get to the hotel front desk after a ten-hour flight. You say:
A "Excuse me, I have a reservation under the name Ito."
B "Hello, I want a room now, please."
C "I am Ito. Give me the key."
The receptionist asks: "Have you checked in online yet?" The best answer is:
A "Yes, I already complete it yet."
B "Yes, I've already completed the online check-in."
C "Yes, I have completed it at three o'clock yesterday."
You want to tell a colleague the story of your arrival, with the times and the sequence. Which one is correct?
A "I have landed at nine and I have taken a cab."
B "I landed at nine, took a cab, and got to the hotel at ten."
C "I land at nine and take a cab yesterday."
Your suitcase has not come out on the belt. You look for an employee and say:
A "My bag hasn't arrived yet. I've already waited twenty minutes at belt six."
B "My bag not arrive. Problem."
C "I don't have bag yet already."
It is nine at night and the room is not ready yet. The most effective reaction is:
A Accept it in silence and wait standing in the lobby, without asking anything.
B "I understand. Could I leave my bags here and have a coffee while I wait?"
C Apologize for your English and give up the room.

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer — speak for 90 seconds, with no script.

It is your first night in New York. You have just arrived at the hotel and the room is not ready yet. Tell the story of your arrival: what time your flight landed, what you did at passport control and at baggage claim (past simple), and what you have already done and what you have not done yet (present perfect with just / already / yet). Finish by asking the front desk two questions. Take your time and don't read from a script.

Survival Card -- Lesson 2

1Excuse me, I have a reservation under the name Ito.
2I've just landed, and I haven't checked in yet.
3I've already completed the online check-in. Here is my confirmation number.
4Could you tell me where the baggage claim is?
5My flight landed at nine, and I took a cab straight to the hotel.
Lesson 03 -- Pre-class

Getting Around and Getting Things Done -- Taxis, Subways, Restaurants, and Shops

The city in motion: a cab on Fifth Avenue, the subway at rush hour, lunch at a diner, and a store where the price at the register is not the price on the tag. Key words: fare, tip, block, subway line, diner, to-go order, sales tax, fitting room, receipt, rush hour, to hail a cab, to figure out. Structure: past simple vs. present perfect in the travel story ("When I went to New York in 2023, I took a cab everywhere -- but this time I've already used the subway twice").
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Take your time — listen twice, then say it out loud. These are the twelve words you use with money in your hand: the taxi, the subway, the check at the restaurant, and the register at the store.

Fare -- the money you pay for a ride in a taxi, a bus or the subway
"How much is the fare to the Upper West Side?"
Tip -- extra money you leave for the person who served you
"In New York, twenty percent is the normal tip at a restaurant."
Block -- the distance from one street corner to the next one
"The subway station is three blocks from the hotel."
Subway line -- one train route in the subway, with a number or a letter
"Take the 1 line uptown and get off at 79th Street."
Diner -- a simple American restaurant that serves breakfast all day
"We had lunch at a diner on Ninth Avenue."
To-go order -- food you take with you instead of eating it at the table
"I'm in a hurry -- could you make it a to-go order?"
Sales tax -- the extra percentage added to the price when you pay at the register
"The price on the tag doesn't include sales tax."
Fitting room -- the small room in a store where you put the clothes on to see if they fit
"Could I try this on? Where is the fitting room?"
Receipt -- the paper that proves you paid -- the P is silent
"Could I have a receipt, please?"
Rush hour -- the time of day when the trains and the streets are full of people
"Don't take the subway at rush hour with a suitcase."
To hail (a cab) -- to raise your hand in the street to stop a taxi
"She hailed a cab on Fifth Avenue and gave the driver the address."
To figure out -- to understand something after some effort -- not immediately
"It took me ten minutes to figure out the subway map."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with the definition that explains it.

Fare
Tip
Block
Subway line
Diner
To-go order
Sales tax
Fitting room
Receipt
Rush hour
To hail (a cab)
To figure out

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text calmly, twice, and then answer the questions.

When Lucimara went to New York in 2023, she took a cab everywhere. She hailed one outside the hotel every morning, she paid the fare in cash, and she never asked for a receipt. That trip is closed: it has a date, so English uses the past simple.

This time is different, and the trip is not over. She has already used the subway twice: the 1 line, uptown, three blocks from the hotel. She has never taken it at rush hour, and honestly she has not figured out the map yet. Yesterday she had lunch at a diner on Ninth Avenue and left a twenty percent tip — that happened, it has a day, so: past simple again. But this afternoon, on Fifth Avenue, she has already tried on a coat in the fitting room, and at the register she learned the New York lesson: the price on the tag is not the price you pay, because of the sales tax.

1. Why is "she went to New York in 2023" in the past simple, and not in the present perfect?
A Because the trip is still happening.
B Because the sentence GIVES THE DATE (in 2023) — the trip is closed. With an explicit date, English needs the past simple.
C Because the result matters more than the moment.
2. "She has already used the subway twice." What does already + present perfect tell you here?
A The trip is STILL OPEN — and so far she has used the subway twice. The number can still go up.
B She used the subway twice in 2023.
C She is going to use the subway twice tomorrow.
3. "She has never taken it at rush hour." Why taken, and not took?
A Because took only exists in British English.
B Because both forms are correct — it makes no difference.
C Because after have / has the verb changes form: the past participle. take → taken, go → gone, eat → eaten.
4. Which sentence is correct?
A "I have gone to New York in 2023 and I have took a cab."
B "I went to New York in 2023 and I took a cab — but this time I've already used the subway."
C "I go to New York in 2023 and I have already take the subway."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Past Simple vs. Present Perfect (the travel story)

GRAMMAR

Two trips, two tenses. One question tells you which one to use.

TenseWhen you use itExample
Past Simple
went · took · paid		A CLOSED moment. You say WHEN it happened (in 2023, last night, yesterday, ten minutes ago), or you tell the sequence of a story."When I went to New York in 2023, I took a cab everywhere."
Present Perfect
have / has + past participle		The trip is still OPEN, or the experience covers your whole life. The moment does NOT matter. Signal words: already, never, ever, so far, this time, twice."This time I've already used the subway twice."
Negativehave / has + not + past participle. In speech: haven't / hasn't."I haven't figured out the subway map yet."
QuestionHave + subject + ever + past participle? (= at any time in your life)"Have you ever tried a New York diner?"
THE PAST PARTICIPLE
(the piece that trips you up)		After have / has, the verb CHANGES FORM: take → taken (never "have took"), go → gone, eat → eaten, pay → paid, buy → bought, do → done.
The one testAsk yourself ONE question: can this number still change today? If it can → present perfect. If it is closed and it has a date → past simple. 2023 is closed. September is still open.

Watch out -- the classic mistake: (1) An explicit date with the present perfect is IMPOSSIBLE: the moment you say in 2023, the verb has to go back to the past simple — "I went", never "I have gone... in 2023". (2) The wrong participle after have: "I have never took" is the mistake that gives your level away. The correct form is "I have never taken".

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence. Tap Listen to hear the whole sentence — as many times as you want.

"In 2023 I a cab from JFK to Midtown."
"This time I have used the subway twice."
"I have never the subway at rush hour."
"How much is the to the Upper West Side?"
"Could I have a , please?"
"The price on the tag doesn't include ."

Stage 2: Put the New York Lunch in Order

Order

One lunch hour in Manhattan. Put the steps in the real order — from the sidewalk to the subway station.

?Order the soup of the day and a coffee.
?Take the receipt and head out to the subway.
?Walk two blocks to the diner on Ninth Avenue.
?Ask for the check and add a twenty percent tip.
?Ask for a table for one and read the specials.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to the sentence, say it out loud, and only then record. Pay attention to what Americans SWALLOW: restaurant has two syllables in their mouth (RES-trant), not three; receipt sounds like ri-SEET, with a silent P; and fare rhymes with air. Saying "less" here is saying it RIGHT.

How much is the fare to the Upper West Side?
When I went to New York in 2023, I took a cab everywhere.
This time I've already used the subway twice.
Could I have the check, please? Is the tip included?
Could I try this on? Where is the fitting room?

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each real moment of a day in New York.

You are on Fifth Avenue with two shopping bags, and you want a taxi. What do you do and say?
A Call the hotel and ask them to send a car — you can never get a taxi on the street.
B Raise your hand to hail one and say: "The Lucerne Hotel, 79th and Amsterdam, please."
C Wait quietly at the curb until someone stops for you.
The driver says the price fast and the street is noisy. The best reaction is:
A Pretend you understood and pay whatever he asks for at the end.
B "Sorry, it's loud out here. Let me read that back to you: about thirty-two dollars. Is that right?"
C "Repeat, repeat!"
You want to tell a colleague about the 2023 trip. Which sentence is correct?
A "I have gone to New York in 2023 and I have took a cab."
B "I went to New York in 2023 and I took a cab everywhere."
C "I go to New York in 2023 and I have already take a cab."
The check arrives at the diner: subtotal $24.00, total $26.13, and the tip line is blank. That means:
A The tip is already in the total, so you do not have to do anything.
B The total already includes the sales tax, but the tip is the part you write in — about 20% of the subtotal.
C There is a mistake on the check and you should complain about it.
In the store on Fifth Avenue you like a coat and you want to try it on. You say:
A "Could I try this on? Where is the fitting room?"
B "I want to use this coat now, please."
C "Where is the room for the clothes?"

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer — speak for 90 seconds, with no script.

Tell the story of one real trip: a taxi you took, a restaurant you went to, or a store you walked into — in New York, in Europe, or in China. Use the past simple for what happened (I took, I went, I paid, I asked). Then compare it with now: what have you already done on this trip, and what have you never done? Finish with one thing you still haven't figured out. Take your time and don't read from a script.

Survival Card -- Lesson 3

1How much is the fare to the Upper West Side?
2When I went to New York in 2023, I took a cab everywhere.
3This time I've already used the subway twice.
4Could I have the check, please? Is the tip included?
5Could I try this on? Where is the fitting room?
Lesson 04 -- Pre-class

If I Miss My Flight -- Handling Problems, Contingencies, and Unexpected Situations

The board turns red in Miami: the flight is canceled, sixty people are in the line, and there is a meeting in Manhattan at nine in the morning. Key words: overbooked, delay notice, to rebook, voucher, compensation, refund, travel insurance, alternative route, complaint, to sort out, to end up, to board. Structure: first conditional — the grammar of plan B ("If the flight is canceled, I will ask for a voucher." / "Unless they rebook me, I’ll end up sleeping at the airport." / "I’ll be fine as long as I board before midnight.").
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Take your time — listen twice, say it out loud. These are the twelve words you will never need — until the night you need all twelve at the same time.

Overbooked -- when a flight or a hotel has sold more seats or rooms than it really has
"The flight is overbooked, so somebody has to take a later one."
Delay notice -- the message that tells you your flight will leave later than planned
"I got the delay notice on my phone before I left the hotel."
To rebook -- to change your reservation to a different flight or a different day
"If they cancel the flight, they will rebook me at no extra cost."
Voucher -- a paper or a code that pays for one thing: a meal, a hotel, a taxi
"The airline gave me a voucher for dinner at the terminal."
Compensation -- extra money a company must pay you because it made you lose time
"After a six-hour delay, you can ask the airline for compensation."
Refund -- your money back, because you did not get the service you paid for
"If they cancel the flight and I don't fly, I will request a refund."
Travel insurance -- a policy that pays you back when the trip goes wrong: illness, lost bags, canceled flights
"My travel insurance covers a night in a hotel near the airport."
Alternative route -- a different way to reach the same place, when the first way is closed
"There is no direct flight tonight, so we will find an alternative route."
Complaint -- a formal statement that says, in writing, that something went wrong
"She filed a complaint with the airline the same night."
To sort out -- to solve a problem, step by step, until it is finished
"Don't worry -- the agent will sort it out before midnight."
To end up -- to be in a situation you did not plan, after everything changed
"I ended up sleeping in a hotel two minutes from the terminal."
To board -- to get on the plane, when they finally call your row
"We will board in twenty minutes at gate B twelve."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with the right definition. Watch the pair that confuses everybody: a refund is YOUR money back; compensation is EXTRA money, for the trouble.

Overbooked
Delay notice
To rebook
Voucher
Compensation
Refund
Travel insurance
Alternative route
Complaint
To sort out
To end up
To board

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text slowly, twice, and then answer the questions.

It is seven in the evening in Miami, and the board turns red. The delay notice arrived an hour ago, and now the flight to New York is canceled. Lucimara does not panic. She builds a plan in her head, and the plan is a sentence: if the line is too long, she will call the airline while she waits.

At the desk, the agent says the next direct flight is overbooked. She does not accept the first no. "If you can get me into JFK before eight, I will take any route," she says. It is an alternative route, through Charlotte, and it works. "I will be fine as long as I am in Manhattan by eight."

The agent rebooks her and gives her a meal voucher and a hotel voucher. There is one condition, and it is the only thing that matters tonight: unless she checks in at the counter before four thirty, the system will release her seat. So she sets three alarms. She ends up eating a bad sandwich at eleven at night, in a terminal she never planned to see, but her travel insurance will cover the hotel.

Tomorrow she will file a complaint, and if the airline agrees, she will get compensation — and a refund for the segment she never flew. But tonight only one thing counts: she sorted it out in English, and she will board at five in the morning.

1. "If you can get me into JFK before eight, I will take any route." Why is the verb after if in the present, if she is talking about the FUTURE?
A Because she is talking about now, not about the future.
B Because that is the iron rule of the first conditional: after if, the verb stays in the PRESENT. Will lives in the OTHER half of the sentence (the result).
C Because the future does not exist in English.
2. "Unless she checks in before four thirty, the system will release her seat." What does unless mean here?
A If not — so: if she does NOT check in by four thirty, she loses her seat. The negative is already INSIDE unless.
B Because — she loses her seat because she checked in.
C Until — she has to wait until four thirty.
3. "I will be fine as long as I am in Manhattan by eight." What does as long as do in this sentence?
A It talks about duration: she will be fine for a long time.
B It sets the ONLY condition that matters: to be in Manhattan by eight. If that happens, the rest (the route, the time of the flight, the hotel) does not matter.
C It compares two flights.
4. Which sentence is correct?
A "If the flight will be canceled, I will ask for a voucher."
B "Unless they don't rebook me, I will file a complaint."
C "If the flight is canceled, I will ask for a voucher — and unless they rebook me tonight, I'll file a complaint."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- First Conditional (the grammar of plan B)

GRAMMAR

One sentence, two halves.

PieceForm / UseExample
The condition
(if...)		if + SIMPLE PRESENT. Never will here — not even when you are talking about tomorrow."If the flight is canceled..."
The resultwill + base verb (no to). In fast speech it becomes ’ll."...I will ask for a voucher." / "I’ll ask."
NegativeIn both halves: doesn’t / don’t in the condition, won’t in the result."If my bag doesn’t arrive, I won’t have a suit for the fair."
QuestionWill + subject + verb + if + present? This is the question you ask at the desk."Will I get a hotel voucher if the flight is canceled?"
unless= if not. The negative is ALREADY inside it — so the verb after it comes in the AFFIRMATIVE."Unless they rebook me, I’ll file a complaint."
as long as
provided that		= the ONLY condition that matters. Same grammar as if: present after it."I’ll be fine as long as I board before midnight."
The order
and the comma		The two halves can change places. Comma only when the condition opens the sentence: "If it is delayed, I will ask." / "I will ask if it is delayed." (no comma)

Watch out -- the classic mistake (there are two, and both come from your first language): (1) will after if: your ear hears a future, so your head writes "if the flight will be delayed". English locks the door: after if, present, always. The will lives in the other half. (2) A double negative with unless: "unless they don’t rebook me" says, word for word, "if they do NOT NOT rebook me" — the opposite of what you wanted to say. Unless already means if not: the verb after it comes in the AFFIRMATIVE.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence. Tap Listen to hear the whole sentence — as many times as you want.

"If the flight canceled, I will ask for a voucher."
"If my flight is delayed, I for compensation."
" they rebook me tonight, I will end up sleeping at the airport."
"I will be fine I board before midnight."
"The airline gave me a for dinner at the terminal."
"If they cancel the flight and I don't fly, I will request a ."

Stage 2: Put the Long Night in Order

Order

The night the flight falls apart. Put the steps in the real order — from the notice on your phone to the alarm in the hotel.

?Take the hotel voucher and find the shuttle at door three.
?Ask the agent for the options and offer a condition: any route before eight.
?Set three alarms -- check-in at the counter closes at four thirty.
?Read the delay notice on your phone and walk to the desk.
?Accept the alternative route through Charlotte and confirm the times out loud.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to the sentence, say it out loud, and only then record. Watch the stressed syllable, which in English breaks the whole word when it lands in the wrong place: voucher (VOW-cher, the VOU rhymes with NOW), compensation (com-pen-SAY-shun), insurance (in-SHOOR-ans), complaint (com-PLAYNT, with an audible final T). And the I’ll almost disappears in fast speech — and that is correct.

My flight has been canceled. What are my options tonight?
If you can get me there before eight, I'll take any route.
Will I get a meal voucher and a hotel voucher for tonight?
Unless there is a seat tonight, I'll end up sleeping at the airport.
I'd like to file a complaint and request compensation.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each real moment of the night the trip breaks.

The board says CANCELED and you reach the desk. What is your FIRST sentence?
A "This is absurd! I have a meeting tomorrow!"
B "Good evening. My flight to New York has been canceled. What are my options tonight?"
C "I want to speak with your manager, please."
The agent says the next direct flight is overbooked. You offer a condition:
A "If you can get me into JFK before eight, I'll take any route."
B "If you will get me into JFK before eight, I take any route."
C "When you get me into JFK, I take any route."
You are going to wait seven hours in Miami. What does the airline OWE you?
A Nothing — a delay is the passenger’s own risk.
B A meal voucher and, with a long wait, a hotel voucher — but you have to ASK for them.
C A free new ticket to any destination in the world.
You did not fly and you want the money for the ticket back. You ask for a:
A compensation — because the airline made you lose time.
B refund — the money for the service you paid for and did not get.
C voucher — a credit to spend at the airport.
Which sentence is correct?
A "Unless they don't rebook me, I will file a complaint."
B "Unless they rebook me tonight, I'll end up sleeping at the airport."
C "Unless they will rebook me, I end up sleeping at the airport."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer — speak for 90 seconds, with no script.

Build your plan B for September, out loud. Your flight to New York is delayed in Miami, and your meeting is at nine the next morning. What will you do? Use at least five conditional sentences: "If the flight is canceled, I will..." / "Unless they rebook me tonight, I will..." / "As long as I land before eight, I will be fine." / "If my bag doesn't arrive, I will..." / "If the hotel asks for a credit card, I will..." Then finish with the real question: what is the one thing that would still scare you at that desk, in English?

Survival Card -- Lesson 4

1My flight has been canceled. What are my options tonight?
2If you can get me there before eight, I'll take any route.
3Will I get a meal voucher and a hotel voucher for tonight?
4Unless there is a seat tonight, I'll end up sleeping at the airport.
5I'd like to file a complaint and request compensation.
Lesson 05 -- Pre-class

What Would You Do? -- Dream Trips, Hypotheticals, and the Week You Would Choose

In Lesson 4 the trip broke and you fixed it. Now the trip goes right — and the question changes: if you had one free week in New York, what would you do with it? Key words: hypothetical, would rather, ideal, budget, to stretch (a budget), red-eye, detour, spontaneous, off the beaten path, to splurge, to cut back, to get away. Structure: second conditional — the grammar of the world that does not exist yet ("If I had a free week in New York, I would walk a different neighborhood every day." / "If I were you, I’d take the ferry.") + I wish + past simple ("I wish I had more time in this city.").
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Take your time — listen twice, then say it out loud. These are the twelve words of someone who is deciding on a trip: what you would do, what you prefer, where you would spend more and where you would spend less.

Hypothetical -- imagined, not real -- a situation you are only supposing
"That is a hypothetical question, but I would say yes."
Would rather -- to prefer one thing to another, when you have to choose
"I would rather arrive early and be tired than lose a whole day."
Ideal -- the best possible version of something, in a perfect world
"In an ideal world, I would spend three days just walking around Brooklyn."
Budget -- the money you plan to spend, decided before you spend it
"We set a budget of three thousand dollars for the week."
To stretch (a budget) -- to make the money go further than you expected
"If we cooked breakfast at the apartment, we would stretch the budget."
Red-eye -- an overnight flight that leaves late and lands early in the morning
"If I took the red-eye, I would gain a whole day in the city."
Detour -- a longer way, off the direct route -- and often the better one
"We took a detour through Brooklyn, and it was the best part of the trip."
Spontaneous -- decided on the spot, with no plan at all
"The best night of the trip was completely spontaneous."
Off the beaten path -- away from the places every tourist goes
"If I had one free day, I would go somewhere off the beaten path."
To splurge -- to spend a lot of money on one special thing, on purpose
"I would splurge on one great dinner, not on the hotel."
To cut back (on) -- to spend or use less of something, so the money lasts
"If we cut back on taxis, we would have more money for restaurants."
To get away -- to travel somewhere to escape your routine, even for a weekend
"I need to get away for a few days -- even a long weekend would help."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with the correct definition. Watch out for the pair that confuses everyone: to splurge is to spend a LOT on purpose, on ONE thing; to stretch a budget is the opposite — to make the same money go further.

Hypothetical
Would rather
Ideal
Budget
To stretch (a budget)
Red-eye
Detour
Spontaneous
Off the beaten path
To splurge
To cut back (on)
To get away

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text slowly, twice, and then answer the questions.

The travel agent asks Lucimara a hypothetical question, and it is the only question that matters: "If you had one free week in New York, what would you do with it?" Nothing is decided yet. The week does not exist. That is exactly why the verb changes.

She does not hesitate. "If I had that week, I would walk," she says. "One neighborhood a day, on foot. In an ideal world, I would spend three days just in Brooklyn — somewhere off the beaten path, not another museum." The agent smiles. "If I were you, I would take the ferry instead of the train. The ferry is a detour, and the detour is where the trip actually happens."

Then the flight. "If you took the red-eye on Saturday, you would land at seven in the morning and you would gain a whole day." She thinks for two seconds. "I would rather arrive early and be tired than lose a Sunday." And the money? "If I cut back on the hotel, I could stretch the budget into two great dinners. I would splurge on the food, never on the room."

One evening stays open, with no plan in it at all. Because the best night of her last trip was completely spontaneous — and you cannot book that. "I wish I had more days and fewer plans," she says. "I wish I were braver when I travel." And then, the real sentence, the one that has nothing hypothetical about it: in September, she is going to get away — and this time she will do it in English.

1. "If I had one free week, I would walk." Why is the verb after if in the PAST, if she is talking about September?
A Because she is telling us about something that already happened.
B Because the past here does not mark TIME, it marks DISTANCE from reality. The free week does not exist yet — it is imagination. That is the rule of the second conditional: if + past simple, would + verb.
C Because in English the future always uses the past.
2. What is the difference between these two sentences: "If my flight is canceled, I will rebook" (Lesson 4) and "If I had a free week, I would walk" (today)?
A The first one is REAL and can happen tomorrow (will). The second one is IMAGINED — a world that does not exist yet (would). The same word if, two different distances.
B There is none — will and would are the same thing.
C The first one is formal and the second one is informal.
3. "If I were you, I would take the ferry." Why were, and not was?
A Because you is plural.
B Because in the second conditional the verb to be becomes were for EVERYONE — I, he, she, it. It is the mark that this is NOT true: I am not you.
C Because was does not exist in American English.
4. Which sentence is correct?
A "If I would have more time, I would visit the MoMA every day."
B "If I had more time, I would visit the MoMA every day — and I wish I had more time."
C "If I have more time, I would visited the MoMA every day."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Second Conditional + I wish (the grammar of the world that does not exist)

GRAMMAR

One sentence, two halves — and only one of them takes would.

PieceForm / UseExample
The condition
(if...)		if + PAST SIMPLE. The past here is not time: it is DISTANCE from reality. Never would in this half."If I had a free week..."
The resultwould + base verb (no to). In fast speech it shrinks to ’d — and almost disappears."...I would walk everywhere." / "I’d walk everywhere."
to be = wereIn the second conditional, to be becomes were for EVERYONE (I / he / she / it). It is the mark of the unreal."If I were you, I’d take the ferry."
Negativedidn’t in the condition, wouldn’t in the result."If I didn’t have the meeting, I wouldn’t stay in Midtown."
QuestionWhat would + subject + verb + if + past simple? This is the question that opens the conversation."What would you do if you had one free week?"
would rather= to prefer (between two options). After it, the verb comes in the BASE form — no to. The contrast uses than."I would rather arrive early than lose a Sunday."
I wish +
past simple		You want NOW to be different. Same trick: past = unreal. It is not about the past, it is about today."I wish I had more time." (= but I do not) / "I wish I were braver."
first x second
(the only question)		Ask yourself one thing: can this really happen? If yes → if + present + will (Lesson 4: "If my flight is canceled, I will rebook."). If not, if it is only imagination → if + past + would ("If I had a free week, I would walk."). The if is the same. What changes is the distance.

Watch out -- the classic mistake (there are two, and both come from your first language): (1) would after if: your head wants a would in BOTH halves — "if I would have more time". English does not allow it: would lives ONLY in the result half. After if, past simple, always. (2) was instead of were: "if I was you" is the most common mistake in the world — and the correct form, the one that sounds like real English, is if I were you. Keep that whole phrase as one block.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence. Tap Listen to hear the whole sentence — as many times as you want.

"If I a free week in New York, I would walk everywhere."
"If I had more time, I the MoMA every day."
"If I you, I would take the ferry instead of the train."
"I arrive early and be tired than lose a whole day."
"I I had more time in this city."
"If I took the , I would gain a whole day in the city."

Stage 2: Build the Perfect Week, in Order

Order

This is how you plan a dream week: in the order the decisions really happen — from the big question to the evening you leave empty.

?Choose where to splurge and where to cut back: the food, not the room.
?Answer the big question: if you had one free week, what would you do?
?Leave one evening completely open -- no plan, on purpose.
?Pick the flight: the red-eye, and gain a whole day in the city.
?Take the ferry instead of the train, and let the detour happen.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to the sentence, say it out loud, and only then record. Today the target is ONE sound: the shrinking would. In an American mouth, I would becomes I’d (one single syllable), she would becomes she’d, we would becomes we’d. And “If I were you, I’d…” comes out glued together, almost like one word. It is not laziness: it is correct English — and it is why you do not hear the would when they speak.

If I had a free week in New York, I would walk a different neighborhood every day.
If I were you, I'd take the ferry instead of the train.
I would rather arrive early and be tired than lose a whole day.
I wish I had more time in this city.
If we cut back on the hotel, we could stretch the budget into two great dinners.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer. The last one is a trap, on purpose: not every if takes a would.

The travel agent asks: “If you had one free week in New York, what would you do?” Which answer is correct?
A "If I have one free week, I will walk a different neighborhood every day."
B "If I had one free week, I would walk a different neighborhood every day."
C "If I would have one free week, I would walk a different neighborhood every day."
You want to say that you prefer the overnight flight to losing a Sunday. You say:
A "I would rather take the red-eye than lose a Sunday."
B "I would rather to take the red-eye than lose a Sunday."
C "I would rather taking the red-eye that lose a Sunday."
The budget is good, but it is not unlimited. You decide to spend a lot on dinner and save on the hotel. You say:
A I would cut back on the dinners and splurge on the room.
B I would splurge on the food and cut back on the hotel — that way we stretch the budget.
C I would stretch the dinners and splurge the budget.
Which sentence is correct?
A "If I was you, I would stay in Brooklyn."
B "If I were you, I would stay in Brooklyn."
C "If I am you, I would stay in Brooklyn."
Careful — this one is REAL, it can happen tomorrow: your flight may be canceled. Which sentence works?
A "If my flight is canceled, I will rebook at the desk."
B "If my flight was canceled, I would rebook at the desk."
C "If my flight would be canceled, I will rebook at the desk."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer — speak for 90 seconds, with no script.

Plan your perfect week in New York, out loud. If you had seven free days in September, and the budget were generous but not unlimited, what would you do each day? Be specific: name a neighborhood, a kind of restaurant, one thing you would do that is off the beaten path. Say where you would splurge and where you would cut back. Then finish with two wishes: "I wish I had..." and "I wish I were..." Take your time and do not read from a script.

Survival Card -- Lesson 5

1If I had a free week in New York, I would walk a different neighborhood every day.
2If I were you, I'd take the ferry instead of the train.
3I would rather arrive early and be tired than lose a whole day.
4I wish I had more time in this city.
5If we cut back on the hotel, we could stretch the budget into two great dinners.
Lesson 06 -- Pre-class

Getting Around Like a Local -- Airports, Transit, and the Rules of the City

In Lesson 5 you chose the perfect week. Now you have to GET there — and the city has rules that nobody explains to you. From the check-in kiosk in Guarulhos to the turnstile in Manhattan. Key words: connecting flight, departure gate, check-in kiosk, rideshare, curbside, turnstile, to tap in, platform, express train, to transfer, uptown, shuttle. Structure: modals of obligation, permission and prohibition — the grammar of a rule ("You have to tap your card before the turnstile." / "You don’t have to buy a ticket." / "You can’t smoke on the platform." / "You are allowed to take a bike, but not at rush hour."). The point that decides everything: don’t have to (it is optional) and can’t (it is forbidden) are OPPOSITES — even though both of them are negative.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Take your time — listen twice, and say it out loud. These are the twelve words that live between the plane and the hotel: half of them are written on a sign, and nobody reads the sign for you.

Connecting flight -- the second flight you take, after the first one lands, on the same trip
"I have a connecting flight in Atlanta, and I have to change terminals."
Departure gate -- the door at the airport where you wait, and where you get on the plane
"You don't have to be at the departure gate two hours early."
Check-in kiosk -- the machine you use to check yourself in, without talking to anybody
"If the line at the counter is long, you can use the check-in kiosk instead."
Rideshare -- a car you order on an app, like Uber or Lyft, instead of a taxi
"At JFK you have to meet your rideshare on the second floor."
Curbside -- the edge of the street, where cars stop to pick you up or drop you off
"Rideshare cars are not allowed to wait at the curbside for more than a minute."
Turnstile -- the metal gate you go through to enter the subway, one person at a time
"You have to tap your card before you go through the turnstile."
To tap in -- to touch your card or your phone on the reader, to pay and go in
"You don't have to buy a ticket anymore -- you can just tap in with your credit card."
Platform -- the long floor next to the tracks, where you stand and wait for the train
"The uptown platform and the downtown platform are on different sides."
Express train -- a train that skips most stations and only stops at the big ones
"The express train doesn't stop at my station, so I have to take the local one."
To transfer -- to get off one train and get on another one, to finish your trip
"You have to transfer at Times Square if you want to go downtown."
Uptown -- toward the north of Manhattan, where the street numbers get bigger
"I was going uptown, but my hotel was downtown -- forty blocks the wrong way."
Shuttle -- a small bus or train that goes back and forth between two places only
"You have to take the shuttle from the terminal to the subway station."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with the correct definition. Watch the pair that catches everybody: the check-in kiosk is the MACHINE (you talk to nobody); the check-in counter from Lesson 2 is the DESK, with a person behind it. In September, the line for one of them has forty people in it, and the line for the other one has two.

Connecting flight
Departure gate
Check-in kiosk
Rideshare
Curbside
Turnstile
To tap in
Platform
Express train
To transfer
Uptown
Shuttle

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text slowly, twice, and then answer the questions.

The line at the counter has forty people in it. The line at the check-in kiosk has two. Lucimara does not have to talk to anybody: she scans her passport, she prints her tag, and she walks to the departure gate. Her connecting flight leaves Atlanta at seven forty.

Eleven hours later, at Kennedy, her rideshare driver calls. He speaks fast, and he says he can’t wait. What nobody told either of them: at that airport, rideshare cars are not allowed to stop at the curbside on the arrivals level. He has to come up to Level 2. So he cancels, and she takes the shuttle to the station instead.

At the station there is a man in a booth. Her suitcase is too big for the turnstile. "You have to use the wide gate," he says, "and you have to tap in there too." She asks whether she has to buy a ticket. She doesn’t. She can just tap the credit card that is already in her hand.

Then she asks the question that saves the whole trip: which platform does she have to be on — uptown or downtown? Both platforms say E. Both trains are the same color. One goes to her hotel in Midtown. The other one is an express train going forty blocks the wrong way, and it would not stop again for a long time. She takes the downtown side. She doesn’t have to transfer at all.

1. "She does not have to talk to anybody." What does that tell you about the counter with a person behind it?
A That she is NOT ALLOWED to talk to the agent at the counter.
B That talking to the agent is OPTIONAL — she can do it if she wants, but she does not need to. Don’t have to = there is no obligation (and nothing stops you).
C That her English is not good enough for that.
2. What is the difference between "he can’t wait" and "she doesn’t have to buy a ticket"?
A They are OPPOSITES. Can’t = forbidden or impossible (the rule stops him). Doesn’t have to = optional (nothing stops her — she simply does not need to).
B No difference — both of them are negative, so they mean the same thing.
C The first one is formal and the second one is informal.
3. "Which platform does she have to be on?" Why is there a does in the question?
A Because every question in English starts with does.
B Because have to is a NORMAL verb — and a normal verb needs do/does to make a question. That is why we say "Do I have to transfer?" and never "Do I must transfer?".
C Because the sentence is in the past.
4. Which sentence is correct?
A "You mustn’t buy a ticket — you can just tap your card." (this FORBIDS the person from buying a ticket!)
B "You don’t have to buy a ticket — you can just tap your card."
C "You don’t must to buy a ticket — you can just tap your card."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Have to / Don’t have to / Can’t / Be allowed to (the grammar of a rule)

GRAMMAR

A city is a set of rules. These are the five ways English says one.

FormUseExample
have to
(affirmative)		OBLIGATION — somebody else’s rule, not your own choice. This is the one an American SAYS (must is the one they write)."You have to tap your card."
mustThe SAME obligation, but you almost always read it: signs, regulations, contracts. Rare in speech."Passengers must hold the handrail." (a sign in the subway)
don’t have to
(negative 1)		NO OBLIGATION — it is OPTIONAL. You can do it if you want to: nothing stops you. This is NOT prohibition."You don’t have to buy a ticket." (you do not need one — but you may)
can’t / must not
(negative 2)		PROHIBITION — it is forbidden. If you do it, there is a consequence."You can’t smoke on the platform."
can /
be allowed to		PERMISSION — the rule says yes. Be allowed to is the more formal one (and the one you see on an official notice)."You are allowed to take a bike, but not at rush hour."
The questionDo I have to…? / Am I allowed to…? — have to is a normal verb: it needs do/does."Do I have to transfer?" — never "Do I must transfer?"
The sound
(the important one)		In an American mouth, have to does NOT sound like have to: it becomes HAFTA, with an F. Has to becomes HASTA. Got to becomes GOTTA. And don’t have to becomes DOAN-HAFTA, all in one piece. That is why you have never heard this grammar — it is everywhere, but it does not sound like the words you learned.

Watch out -- the classic mistake: both of them are negative, so the mind glues them together into one thing. In English they are OPPOSITES: don’t have to SETS YOU FREE (it is optional), mustn’t / can’t STOPS YOU (it is forbidden). If you tell a tourist "you mustn’t buy a ticket", you have just forbidden her to buy one. The question that always solves it: does this sentence FREE me, or does it STOP me?

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence. Tap Listen to hear the whole sentence — as many times as you want. Pay attention to the sound: have to is going to sound like HAFTA.

"You tap your card before you go through the turnstile."
"You buy a ticket -- you can just tap your credit card."
"You smoke on the platform."
"Am I to take this suitcase through the gate?"
"You have to at 42nd Street to go downtown."
"The train doesn't stop at my station, so I have to take the local one."

Stage 2: The Sixty-Two Minutes, in Order

Order

From the kiosk in Guarulhos to the right platform in New York — the whole trip, in the order it really happens.

?Take the shuttle from the terminal to the subway station.
?Use the check-in kiosk, and skip the line of forty people at the counter.
?Tap in at the wide gate, because the suitcase does not fit through the turnstile.
?Walk to the departure gate and board the connecting flight.
?Check the platform -- downtown, not uptown -- and get on the express train.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to the sentence, say it out loud, and only then record. Today the target is ONE sound: have to, glued together. In an American mouth, have to becomes HAFTA (with an F!), has to becomes HASTA, and don’t have to becomes DOAN-HAFTA, all in one piece. If it comes out as "have · to", separate, it is wrong — even when it is correct. It is not laziness: it is real English — and it is exactly why you do not hear the obligation when they speak.

Excuse me, do I have to transfer to get to Midtown?
Which platform do I have to be on -- uptown or downtown?
You don't have to buy a ticket -- you can just tap your credit card.
Am I allowed to take this suitcase through the gate?
Let me check I understood: the E train, downtown platform, no transfer.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer. Question 3 is the trap of this lesson: not every negative sentence is a "no".

You have a big suitcase and the turnstile is too narrow for it. You ask the station agent:
A "Do I must use another gate?"
B "Do I have to use another gate? This suitcase doesn't fit through the turnstile."
C "I am allowed use another gate?"
Two platforms, the same letter, the same color. You have four seconds. The question that saves the trip is:
A "Which platform do I have to be on -- uptown or downtown?"
B "How do I get to Midtown?" (and then comes a fifteen-second answer that you do not understand)
C "Where is the train?"
A tourist behind you is in line at the machine, about to buy a ticket she does not need. You want to SET HER FREE. You say:
A "You mustn't buy a ticket -- you can just tap your card." (this FORBIDS her from buying one!)
B "You don't have to buy a ticket -- you can just tap your credit card."
C "You can't buy a ticket -- you can just tap your card."
Your rideshare driver is waiting on Level 1, where pickup is forbidden, and the police are moving the cars along. You say:
A "You don't have to wait on Level 1 -- you have to come up to Level 2."
B "You can't wait on Level 1 -- you have to come up to Level 2. Pickup is only allowed at the curbside upstairs."
C "You are not allowed come up to Level 2."
The agent gave you the line, the platform and the transfer, all very fast. Before you walk away, you:
A Say thank you and walk away, hoping that you understood him.
B "Let me check I understood: the E train, downtown platform, no transfer. Is that right?"
C "Sorry, can you repeat everything again from the beginning?" (and he repeats it just as fast)

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer — speak for 90 seconds, with no script.

Explain the rules of your own factory in Guarulhos to a foreign visitor who has never been there. What does he have to do before he goes in? What does he NOT have to do (careful -- this is the hard one: it means it is optional, not forbidden)? And what is he not allowed to do, under any circumstances? Use have to, don’t have to, can’t and be allowed to. This is tonight’s grammar on the ground where you are the expert -- so take your time, and do not read from a script.

Survival Card -- Lesson 6

1Excuse me, do I have to transfer to get to Midtown?
2Which platform do I have to be on -- uptown or downtown?
3You don't have to buy a ticket -- you can just tap your credit card.
4Am I allowed to take this suitcase through the gate?
5Let me check I understood: the E train, downtown platform, no transfer.
Lesson 07 -- Pre-class

Speaking Up with Confidence -- Hotels, Reservations, and When Things Go Wrong

In Lesson 6 the city gave you the rules. Now the hotel gives you nothing — you have to ask for what you want, and in English, HOW you ask changes the answer. Key words: amenities, suite, housekeeping, valet, surcharge, late checkout, an upgrade, room service, a wake-up call, a deposit, spotless. Grammar: polite requests and indirect questions — the difference between an order and a favor ("Could you send someone up?" / "Would you mind moving me to a quieter room?" / "I was wondering if you could check the bill." / "Do you know if the room is ready?"). Firm, never rude — the guest people want to help.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Take your time — listen twice, then say it out loud. These are the eleven words of a hotel front desk: the room, the extras, and everything that shows up when something needs fixing.

Amenities -- the extra services and comforts a hotel offers
"The hotel has great amenities: a gym, a spa, and a rooftop pool."
Suite -- a large hotel room with a separate sitting area
"They moved me to a junior suite that overlooks the park."
Housekeeping -- the hotel team that cleans and prepares the rooms
"Housekeeping finishes the upper floors by one in the afternoon."
Valet -- a hotel worker who parks and brings your car for you
"There is a valet at the door, but I did not bring a car."
Surcharge -- an extra charge added on top of the normal price
"There was a valet surcharge on my bill, and I never used the parking."
Late checkout -- permission to leave your room later than the usual time
"Could I have a late checkout? My flight is not until the evening."
An upgrade -- a better room than the one you originally booked
"They gave me a free upgrade to a room with a park view."
Room service -- food and drinks brought up to your room
"It was late, so I ordered room service instead of going out."
A wake-up call -- a phone call the hotel makes to wake you at a set time
"Could I have a wake-up call at six? I have an early meeting."
A deposit -- money you pay in advance and get back later if nothing is wrong
"They take a deposit at check-in and return it when you leave."
Spotless -- perfectly clean, with nothing dirty at all
"The room was spotless -- I could not find a single thing out of place."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with the correct definition. Watch out for the pair that confuses everyone: a surcharge is money you did not expect to pay and often should not; a deposit is money you pay in advance and get back.

Amenities
Suite
Housekeeping
Valet
Surcharge
Late checkout
An upgrade
Room service
A wake-up call
A deposit
Spotless

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text slowly, twice, and then answer the questions.

Lucimara reaches the front desk a little early. She does not say "give me my room." She says, "Do you know if the room is ready yet? I'm happy to wait." Notice the shape: inside the question, the words stay in normal order — if the room is ready, not is the room ready. It is a question wearing the clothes of a statement, and that is exactly what makes it sound polite.

The clerk has good news. They have given her an upgrade — a junior suite, already cleaned by housekeeping, spotless and quiet. But there is a surcharge on the reservation for valet parking, and Lucimara did not bring a car. She does not get angry. She opens softly: "I was wondering if you could take a look at this. There seems to be a valet charge, and I didn't use the parking." Same message as "this is wrong" — but now they are on the same side of the desk.

Then the small favors. "Would you mind adding a wake-up call for six?" she asks — and after would you mind, the verb always takes -ing, never to add. "And could I possibly have a late checkout on Friday? My flight is in the evening." The clerk cannot promise the late checkout, but he flags it. He takes a refundable deposit, removes the surcharge, and thanks her.

Nothing she asked for was unreasonable. What made the difference was not the words — it was the wrapper around them. She asked like someone who expected a yes, and she got one. The amenities were the same for every guest; the upgrade, the removed charge, the flagged late checkout were not. They went to the guest who was calm, clear, and kind.

1. "Do you know if the room is ready?" Why is it the room is and not is the room?
A Because it is a mistake — questions always invert the verb.
B Because it is an indirect question: the real question is "do you know...?", and inside it the words keep normal statement order. This is what makes it sound polite.
C Because "the room is" is more formal than "is the room".
2. "Would you mind adding a wake-up call?" Why adding and not to add?
A After would you mind, the verb always takes -ing. It is a fixed rule: would you mind waiting / sending / moving.
B Because add has no infinitive.
C Because adding is in the past tense.
3. Lucimara had a wrong charge on her bill. What made her complaint work?
A She raised her voice so the clerk would take her seriously.
B She opened softly ("I was wondering if you could...") and then stated the fact clearly ("I didn't use the parking"). Kind frame, firm content.
C She apologized for the charge and paid it anyway.
4. Which request is correct?
A "Would you mind to move me to another room?"
B "Would you mind moving me to another room? And could you tell me what time checkout is?"
C "Would you mind moving me, and could you tell me what time is checkout?"

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Polite Requests and Indirect Questions

GRAMMAR

Same request, different wrappers. The bigger the favor, the softer you go.

WrapperForm / UseExample
Could you...?Could you + base verb. The everyday polite request. Neutral, works everywhere."Could you send someone up, please?"
Would you mind...?Would you mind + verb-ing. Softer. Careful: "Sure" or "Not at all" means yes, I'll do it."Would you mind moving me to a quieter room?"
I was wondering if...I was wondering if you could + base verb. The softest — keep it for a bigger favor."I was wondering if you could check the bill for me."
Indirect questionDo you know if / Could you tell me + normal word order inside. No inversion, no do/does."Do you know if the room is ready?" / "Could you tell me what time checkout is?"
Softening a complaintThere seems to be + problem. It states the fact without accusing anyone."There seems to be a surcharge here, and I didn't use the parking."
The one ideaEvery wrapper does the same job: it leaves the other person a way to say yes. You are not weaker for being polite — the soft version is the one that gets the upgrade, the late checkout, the charge removed.

Watch out -- the two classic mistakes: (1) would you mind to move — after would you mind the verb always ends in -ing: would you mind moving. (2) The inverted indirect question: "do you know where is the gym?" — inside an indirect question the words stay in normal order: "do you know where the gym is?". Learn those two, and you sound polished, not just correct.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence. Tap Listen to hear the whole sentence — as many times as you want.

" send someone up to look at the heating?"
"Would you mind me to a quieter room?"
"Do you know if the room ready yet?"
"I was wondering if you could the bill for me."
"There to be a surcharge, and I didn't use the parking."
"Could you tell me what time ?"

Stage 2: Handle the Problem, in Order

Order

This is how you speak up about a wrong charge and win: in the order that actually works — from the soft opening to the calm confirmation.

?Make the request: "Would you mind taking it off my bill?"
?Open softly, not with the problem: "I was wondering if you could help me."
?Confirm at the end: "Let me check I got that -- the charge is gone. Is that right?"
?State the fact clearly: "There seems to be a valet charge, and I didn't bring a car."
?Stay firm if they hesitate: "I understand, but I really didn't use the parking."

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to the sentence, say it out loud, and only then record. Today the target is the melody of a polite ask: let your voice rise gently at the end, and slow down. The same words said flat sound like an order; said with the rising tune, they sound like a favor.

Do you know if the room is ready yet?
Would you mind sending someone up to look at the heating?
I was wondering if you could take this charge off my bill.
Could I possibly have a late checkout on Friday?
There seems to be a surcharge here, and I didn't use the parking.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer. Some options are grammatically wrong; others are just too blunt for the moment.

You arrive early and want to know if you can check in. Which sounds best?
A "Is the room ready? Give me my key."
B "Do you know if the room is ready yet? I'm happy to wait if it isn't."
C "Do you know is the room ready?"
The heating is broken. You call the front desk. You say:
A "Would you mind to send someone to fix the heating?"
B "Would you mind sending someone up to look at the heating?"
C "You must send someone to fix the heating now."
There is a valet surcharge on your bill, but you brought no car. How do you speak up?
A "I was wondering if you could take a look at this — there seems to be a valet charge, and I didn't use the parking."
B "This charge is a lie. Remove it right now."
C "Sorry, sorry, it's probably fine, never mind."
You want to know what time breakfast starts. Which is correct?
A "Could you tell me what time does breakfast start?"
B "Could you tell me what time breakfast starts?"
C "Could you tell me what time starts breakfast?"
Your flight is late on Friday and you want to stay in the room longer. You ask:
A "I need a late checkout. Make it happen."
B "Could I possibly have a late checkout on Friday? My flight is not until the evening."
C "Do you know can I have a late checkout?"

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer — speak for about 90 seconds, with no script.
