You are at a hotel front desk, and your bill has two charges that are wrong: a valet fee for a car you never had, and a minibar charge for a minibar you never opened. Speak up, out loud, as if the clerk were in front of you. Open politely, name both problems with the facts, ask for them to be removed, and confirm at the end. Use at least two of today's wrappers: "I was wondering if you could...", "Would you mind...", "There seems to be...", "Could you tell me...". Be firm and kind at the same time. Take your time and do not read from a script.

Survival Card -- Lesson 7

1Do you know if the room is ready yet?
2Would you mind sending someone up to look at it?
3I was wondering if you could help me sort out this charge.
4There seems to be a surcharge here, and I didn't use the parking.
5Could I possibly have a late checkout on Friday?
Lesson 08 -- Pre-class

Eating Out with Confidence -- Restaurants, Orders, and Recommendations

In Lesson 7 you learned to speak up at the hotel. Now you sit down to eat — and this time nothing is wrong: you are the one hosting. From the first appetizer to the last thing you sign. Key words: appetizer, entree, side dish, dietary restriction, gluten-free, medium-rare, complimentary, tab, sommelier, tipping etiquette. Structure: recommendations and suggestions — the grammar of offering an idea ("I’d recommend the sea bass." / "You might want to try the tasting menu." / "Why don’t you start with the soup?" / "Should I mention the dietary restriction now?"). The rule that catches everybody: after recommend comes a noun or -ing ("recommend the salmon" / "recommend trying it"), never "to try".
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Take your time — listen twice, and say it out loud. These are the ten words that live between the first course and the last thing you sign at an American restaurant.

Appetizer -- the small dish you eat first, before the main course
"Why don’t we share an appetizer? The soup here is very good."
Entree -- the main course of the meal -- the big plate, in American English
"For my entree, I’ll go with the steak, medium-rare."
Side dish -- a smaller dish that comes next to the main one, like vegetables or fries
"Could I get a side dish of grilled vegetables with the steak?"
Dietary restriction -- a food someone cannot or must not eat, for health, allergy or belief
"My guest has a dietary restriction — he can’t eat gluten."
Gluten-free -- made without wheat or gluten -- safe for someone who can’t eat it
"Do you have any gluten-free options for the main course?"
Medium-rare -- cooked so the meat is still pink and warm in the middle
"I’ll have the steak, medium-rare, please."
Complimentary -- given free by the restaurant, as a gift, with nothing to pay
"The bread and olive oil are complimentary — on the house."
Tab -- the bill -- the paper that shows how much you owe at the end
"Could we have the tab, please? It’s on me tonight."
Sommelier -- the restaurant expert who helps you choose the wine
"The sommelier recommended a light red with the fish."
Tipping etiquette -- the polite rules about how much extra money you leave after a meal
"American tipping etiquette is eighteen to twenty percent, added by you."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with the correct definition. Watch the false friend of the night: in the U.S., an entree is the MAIN course, not the starter — and complimentary means free, not a compliment.

Appetizer
Entree
Side dish
Dietary restriction
Gluten-free
Medium-rare
Complimentary
Tab
Sommelier
Tipping etiquette

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text slowly, twice, and then answer the questions.

Lucimara arrives first, on purpose, to read the menu before her guest sits down. When the server brings a small plate of warm bread, he says it is complimentary — on the house. She sees the appetizers on the left, the entrees in the middle, and a short list of side dishes at the bottom.

When Mr. Chen sits down, she does not hand him the menu in silence. "Why don’t you start with the soup?" she says. "And you might want to try the sea bass — I’d recommend it. Marco says it is the best thing tonight."

There is one thing she must get right. Mr. Chen has a dietary restriction: he is gluten-free. So before they order, she turns to the server. "Should I mention it to the kitchen now?" Yes, always now, he tells her — better asked early than sent back later. For herself, she orders the steak, medium-rare, and a side of vegetables.

At the end, the tab arrives in a small folder. In the United States, the tip is not printed on the bill; you add eighteen to twenty percent yourself. She knows the tipping etiquette now. "It’s on me," she tells Mr. Chen, before he can reach for his wallet.

1. "I’d recommend it." After recommend, which is correct?
A "I’d recommend to try the sea bass." (never "to" after recommend)
B "I’d recommend the sea bass." or "I’d recommend trying the sea bass." (a noun, or the -ing form)
C "I’d recommend that you trying it."
2. "Why don’t you start with the soup?" What is Lucimara doing here?
A Ordering her guest to eat the soup — it is a command.
B Making a friendly SUGGESTION as a question — her guest is still free to choose something else. Note: after why don’t you, the base verb (start), no "to".
C Asking a real question about the reason she does not eat soup.
3. The bread is complimentary. What does that mean for the bill?
A It is free — the restaurant gives it as a gift, and it does not appear on the tab.
B It is a compliment from the chef, and you pay extra for it.
C It is the most expensive thing on the menu.
4. "Should I mention it to the kitchen now?" Why does Lucimara ask BEFORE ordering?
A Because you are not allowed to eat gluten in the United States.
B Because a dietary restriction is easier to plan early than to fix on the plate — and Should I...? is how you ask for advice.
C Because the kitchen closes before the meal starts.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Recommendations and Suggestions (the grammar of offering an idea)

GRAMMAR

A host does not give orders. A host offers ideas. These are the four ways English does it.

OpenerFormExample
I’d recommend...I’d recommend + a NOUN, or + verb-ing. Never "to"."I’d recommend the salmon." / "I’d recommend trying it."
You might want to...You might want to + base verb. The "to" belongs to want to and stays."You might want to try the tasting menu."
Why don’t you...?Why don’t you + base verb. A suggestion in the shape of a question. No "to"."Why don’t you start with the soup?"
Should I...? / Shall I...?Should I + base verb. Use it to ASK for a recommendation, or to offer to do something."Should I mention the dietary restriction now?"
Asking for oneWhat do you recommend? — your best defense against a fast menu."What would you recommend? It’s my first time here."
The one ruleAfter recommend, a noun or -ing (never "to try"). After why don’t you and should I, the base verb, no "to". The word "to" only survives inside want to. Each opener OFFERS an idea — the other person can always say no.

Watch out -- the classic mistake: the mind translates "recomendo experimentar" and puts an infinitive: "I’d recommend to try". In English that is wrong. It is "I’d recommend trying" or "I’d recommend the salmon". Remember: recommend takes a noun or an -ing, and it never takes "to".

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence. Tap Listen to hear the whole sentence — as many times as you want. Watch the word after each opener: it is never "to".

"I’d the sea bass — it’s the best thing tonight."
"You might try the tasting menu."
"Why start with the soup?"
" mention his dietary restriction to the kitchen now?"
"The bread is — we don’t pay for it."
"Could we have the , please? It’s on me tonight."

Stage 2: The Dinner, in Order

Order

From sitting down to picking up the tab — the whole dinner, in the order it really happens.

?Recommend two dishes to your guest before he decides.
?Sit down and read the menu — the bread is complimentary.
?Order the entrees, and ask the sommelier for a wine.
?Tell the server your guest is gluten-free, before ordering.
?Ask for the tab, add the tip, and say "it’s on me".

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to the sentence, say it out loud, and only then record. Watch the tricky words: entree is "ON-tray", sommelier is "so-mel-YAY", and medium-rare is said as one word.

What would you recommend? It’s my first time here.
I’d recommend the sea bass — why don’t you start with the soup?
My guest is gluten-free — should I mention it to the kitchen now?
I’ll have the steak, medium-rare, with a side dish of vegetables.
Could we have the tab, please? It’s on me — and I’ll add the tip.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer. Question 1 is the trap: after "recommend", the word is never "to".

You loved the fish here last time. You want to recommend it to your guest. You say:
A "I’d recommend to try the sea bass."
B "I’d recommend the sea bass — I had it last time, and it was excellent."
C "I recommend you for try the sea bass."
The menu is a wall of words you half understand. The best move is:
A "What would you recommend? It’s my first time here." (short answer, easy to catch)
B Point at something and hope it is what you wanted.
C Read the whole menu out loud until you find a word you know.
Your guest cannot eat gluten. Before you order, you turn to the server and say:
A "He is not allowed to eat here." (that is not what a dietary restriction means)
B "He’s gluten-free — should I mention it to the kitchen now?"
C Say nothing, and hope the main course has no gluten.
The warm bread arrives before you order. You are not sure if you pay for it. It is:
A Complimentary — free, on the house. It will not be on the tab.
B An appetizer you have to pay for separately.
C The most expensive item, added to the tab automatically.
The tab arrives, and your Chinese supplier reaches for his wallet. As the host, you:
A Let him pay — and forget that in the U.S. you add the tip yourself.
B "Please, it’s on me." — said early, and then you add eighteen to twenty percent for the tip.
C Wait, because the tip is already printed on the bill in America.

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer — speak for 90 seconds, with no script.

You are hosting your supplier from Shanghai at a restaurant in New York. He has never had American food, and he is gluten-free. Recommend a starter and a main course to him, using at least three different openers (I’d recommend..., you might want to..., why don’t you...?, should I...?). Then explain how you will handle the tab and the tip at the end. This is tonight’s grammar in the situation you will really live in September — so take your time, and do not read from a script.

Survival Card -- Lesson 8

1What would you recommend? It’s my first time here.
2I’d recommend the sea bass — why don’t you start with the soup?
3My guest is gluten-free — should I mention it to the kitchen now?
4I’ll have the steak, medium-rare, with a side dish of vegetables.
5Could we have the tab, please? It’s on me — and I’ll add the tip.
Lesson 09 -- Pre-class

Shopping, Returning, and Negotiating Price -- Being Direct and Polite in American Stores

In Lesson 8 you were a guest at the table. Now you are a customer at the register — and the money is yours. Key words: exchange, defective, return policy, warranty, cashier, markdown, clearance, price match, restocking fee, store credit, loyalty program, size conversion. Grammar: used to — a habit that was true then and is not true now ("I used to shop only in Brazil." / "I didn't use to feel comfortable returning things." / "Did you use to keep every receipt?"). Direct, never rude — and no apologizing when the item is defective and you are in the right.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Take your time — listen twice, then say it out loud. These are the twelve words of a store counter: returning, exchanging, and getting the right price.

Exchange -- to give an item back and get a different one instead
"I'd like to exchange this for a larger size, please."
Defective -- broken or not working the way it should
"The zipper is broken -- the jacket is defective."
Return policy -- the store's written rules about giving items back
"Their return policy gives you thirty days with a receipt."
Warranty -- a written promise to repair or replace a product if it breaks
"The blender has a two-year warranty, so they fixed it for free."
Cashier -- the person at the register who takes your payment
"The cashier scanned the tag and told me the sale price."
Markdown -- a reduction in an item's price
"There was a fifty percent markdown on the coats after the holidays."
Clearance -- a section of heavily discounted items the store wants to clear out
"I found these boots on the clearance rack for almost nothing."
Price match -- when a store agrees to match a lower price from a competitor
"They did a price match once I showed them the cheaper website."
Restocking fee -- a charge some stores take when you return certain items
"There was a fifteen percent restocking fee on the returned TV."
Store credit -- money that can only be spent at that same store
"Without a receipt, they only gave me store credit, not cash."
Loyalty program -- a rewards plan that gives regular customers points or discounts
"Their loyalty program gave me ten dollars off after five visits."
Size conversion -- changing a size from one country's system to another
"I needed a size conversion -- a Brazilian 38 is a US small."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with the correct definition. Watch the pair that confuses everyone: a refund puts money back on your card, but store credit can only be spent at that same store.

Exchange
Defective
Return policy
Warranty
Cashier
Markdown
Clearance
Price match
Restocking fee
Store credit
Loyalty program
Size conversion

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text slowly, twice, and then answer the questions.

Lucimara used to keep everything she bought abroad, even when it was wrong. If a jacket did not fit, it stayed in the closet with the tags on. She didn't use to know that she could walk back into the store and return it. Notice the shape: used to for the old habit, and the little word -d is there — used to, not use to.

This time is different. The zipper on her new jacket is broken, so it is defective. She reads the return policy first: thirty days with the receipt, but for a defective item, no time limit at all. She takes it to the cashier and says, calmly, "This is defective — I'd like to exchange it." Because it is broken, there is no restocking fee.

Then she notices a bag she bought is cheaper on another website. "Do you do a price match?" she asks. They do. She also has no receipt for a second item, so for that one they can only give her store credit, not cash. On the way out she passes the clearance rack, full of big markdowns, and remembers her loyalty program points.

A year ago, none of this would have happened. She didn't use to ask questions, and she used to pay whatever the tag said. What changed was not the stores — it was her. She learned the words, and she learned that a defective item is a right, not a favor.

1. "I used to shop only in Brazil." What does this tell us about now?
A She still shops only in Brazil.
B She does not shop only in Brazil anymore. Used to means a past habit that is no longer true.
C She will shop only in Brazil in the future.
2. Which sentence is correct?
A "I didn't used to keep my receipts."
B "I didn't use to keep my receipts." After did / didn't, the -d disappears.
C "I didn't use to keeping my receipts."
3. The scarf is marked "final sale," but it has a hole. What can she do?
A Nothing — "final sale" always means no returns.
B Return it — a defective item can be returned even on final sale, with no restocking fee.
C Keep it and hope it gets better.
4. She has no receipt for an item. What will the store most likely give her?
A A full cash refund, no questions asked.
B Store credit — money she can only spend at that same store.
C A price match on a different item.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- "Used to" for Past Habits

GRAMMAR

Used to talks about a habit or a state that was true in the past and is not true anymore. It always contrasts then with now.

FormStructureExample
Affirmativeused to + base verb (keep the -d)I used to pay full price.
Negativedidn't use to + base verb (no -d)I didn't use to check the return policy.
QuestionDid + subject + use to + base verb (no -d)Did you use to keep every receipt?
The contrastused to (then) + now + present simpleI used to keep things that didn't fit; now I exchange them.
The one ideaused to = a past habit that is not true anymore. The verb after it is always the base form — never -ing, never past.

Watch out -- the two classic mistakes: (1) dropping the -d in the affirmative: "I use to shop..." — it is always "I used to shop..." (2) keeping the -d after did / didn't: "Did you used to...?" — the did already carries the past, so it is "Did you use to...?". Learn those two, and your "used to" sounds native.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence. Tap Listen to hear the whole sentence — as many times as you want.

"I pay full price, but now I wait for a markdown."
"I didn't know I could return a defective item."
"The zipper is broken, so it's ."
"Do you do a ? I found it cheaper online."
"I don't have the receipt, so they can only give me ."
"Could I this for a larger size?"

Stage 2: Return It, in Order

Order

This is how you return a defective item and get what you are owed — in the order that actually works, from stating the problem to confirming at the end.

?Say what you want: "I'd like a refund, or an exchange for the same one."
?State the problem clearly: "I'd like to return this — it's defective."
?Confirm at the end: "So the exchange is free, with no restocking fee. Is that right?"
?Show the proof: "Here's the receipt — I bought it yesterday."
?Hold your ground if needed: "Your policy covers defective items, even on final sale."

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to the sentence, say it out loud, and only then record. Today, watch two things: the silent p in "receipt" (ri-SEET), and the difference between saying a fact firmly and apologizing for it.

I'd like to return this — it's defective. Here's the receipt.
Could I exchange this for a different size?
Do you do a price match? I found it cheaper online.
I understand it's final sale, but it's defective.
I used to keep things that didn't fit; now I exchange them.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer. Some options are grammatically wrong; others are just too blunt or too weak for the moment.

Your new headphones don't work. You're at the counter with the receipt. You say:
A "Sorry to bother you, it's probably my fault, never mind."
B "I'd like to return these — they're defective. Here's the receipt."
C "These are broken. Give me my money now."
You want the same shirt in a bigger size, not your money back. Which is correct?
A "Could I refund this for a large?"
B "Could I exchange this for a large?"
C "Could I store credit this for a large?"
Talking about how your shopping habits changed. Which sentence is correct?
A "I use to pay full price, but now I wait for a markdown."
B "I used to pay full price, but now I wait for a markdown."
C "I used to paying full price, but now I wait for a markdown."
The scarf is "final sale," but it has a hole in it. What do you say?
A "I understand it's final sale, but it's defective — there's a hole. Your policy covers that, so I'd like a refund."
B "Okay, if it's final sale, I'll just keep it."
C "This store is a scam. I want the manager right now."
You found the same speaker cheaper on another store's website. You ask:
A "Do you do a restocking fee? It's cheaper online."
B "Do you do a price match? I found this exact model for less online."
C "Do you do a size conversion on the price?"

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer — speak for about 90 seconds, with no script.

Talk about how you used to shop, and how you shop now. Use "used to" and "didn't use to" at least twice each. Then imagine you are at a store counter with a defective item and no help on the screen: state the problem, ask for an exchange or a refund, ask about a price match, and confirm at the end. Be direct and polite — and remember, with a defective item you are not asking for a favor. Take your time and do not read from a script.

Survival Card -- Lesson 9

1I'd like to return this -- it's defective. Here's the receipt.
2Could I exchange this for a different size?
3Do you do a price match? I found it cheaper online.
4I understand it's final sale, but it's defective.
5Is this your best price?
Lesson 10 -- Pre-class

Talking About Your Work -- Explaining Your Role and Industry with Confidence

In Lesson 9 you learned to be direct and polite in a store. Now the subject is YOU — your company, your industry, your work. This is the lesson for the Lyon fair in November and the New York dinner in September, when someone asks, "So, what does your company do?" Key words: regulatory compliance, supply chain, raw material, manufacturing process, quality control, distributor, vendor, batch, certification, shipment. Structure: the passive voice — the grammar business runs on ("Our products are tested before distribution." / "Regulations are set by the health authority." / "The formula was approved last year."). The rule that catches everybody: after be, always the past participle ("are tested", never "are test"), and passive always needs the be verb.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Take your time — listen twice, and say it out loud. These are the ten words that describe your own industry, from the raw material to the final shipment. You already know them in Portuguese; today they get their English version.

Regulatory compliance -- following the rules and laws set by an authority
"In our market, no product is sold without full regulatory compliance."
Supply chain -- the whole path a product takes, from raw material to the customer
"Our supply chain reaches Europe, so we can deliver to Lyon."
Raw material -- the basic material a product is made from, before it is processed
"Most of our raw materials are sourced from vendors in Asia."
Manufacturing process -- the series of steps used to make a product in a factory
"The whole manufacturing process is monitored by our team."
Quality control -- the checks that make sure a product is good and safe before it is sold
"Every batch passes quality control before it leaves the plant."
Distributor -- a company that buys products and sells them on to stores or clients
"Daniel's company could become our distributor in France."
Vendor -- a company or person that sells goods or services to another company
"We source our raw materials from three trusted vendors."
Batch -- a quantity of a product made together at one time, as one group
"Every batch is tested before it is released to the market."
Certification -- an official document that proves a product or plant meets a standard
"The plant holds a certification to international standards."
Shipment -- a load of goods sent from one place to another
"The first shipment could be arranged for early next year."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with the correct definition. Watch the pair that is easy to mix up: a vendor sells TO your company, while a distributor buys FROM you and sells on to clients.

Regulatory compliance
Supply chain
Raw material
Manufacturing process
Quality control
Distributor
Vendor
Batch
Certification
Shipment

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text slowly, twice, and then answer the questions. Notice how many sentences use the passive voice.

At the Lyon fair, a man stops at Lucimara's booth and asks what her company does. This time she does not point at the brochure. "We specialize in specialty chemicals," she says. "Our products are manufactured in Guarulhos, and every batch is tested before it is released."

The man is interested. "And your supply chain? Where are the raw materials sourced?" Lucimara answers calmly, because she knows the words. "Most of them are sourced from vendors in Asia and Europe, and each shipment is checked when it arrives."

In his market, he explains, a product cannot be sold without proper certification and full regulatory compliance. Lucimara nods. "Every regulation in our sector is set by the health authority, and each one is followed. Last year the formula was approved, and our certification was renewed."

The man hands her his card. He is a distributor across France and Belgium, and he would like to talk. Two minutes, one language, and a booth she used to dread — and the whole manufacturing process of her company, explained in English.

1. "Our products are manufactured in Guarulhos." How is the passive voice built here?
A manufacture + -ing (the continuous form)
B a form of be (are) + the past participle (manufactured) — the product is the star, not the person.
C just the base verb: "Our products manufacture in Guarulhos."
2. "The formula was approved last year." Why was and not is?
A Because the passive voice always uses "was".
B Because it is a finished action in the PAST — past passive is was/were + past participle.
C Because "formula" is a plural word.
3. "Every regulation is set by the health authority." Why is "by" used here?
A Because WHO sets the rules matters — you add "by + the doer" only when the doer is important.
B Because every passive sentence must end with "by".
C Because "by" means the same as "from" for a place.
4. A vendor and a distributor — what is the difference in the text?
A They are two words for the same company.
B A vendor SELLS the raw materials to Lucimara; a distributor would BUY her products and sell them on across France and Belgium.
C A vendor is a customer, and a distributor is a factory.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- The Passive Voice (the grammar business runs on)

GRAMMAR

In business, we care about the process, not the person. The passive voice lets you talk about what is done, without naming who does it.

StructureFormExample
Present passiveam / is / are + past participle"Our products are tested before distribution."
Past passivewas / were + past participle"The formula was approved last year."
The "by"… by + the doer — only when who does it matters"Regulations are set by the health authority."
The place… from / in + a place (not "by")"The materials are sourced from vendors in Asia."
The one rulePassive = a form of be (am/is/are/was/were) + the past participle (test → tested, set → set, approve → approved). Choose the be for the time (present or past), and never drop the -ed.

Watch out -- the classic mistakes: (1) dropping the participle — "Our products are test" should be "are tested". (2) dropping the be verb — "The formula approved last year" should be "was approved". Without be, there is no passive.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence. Tap Listen to hear the whole sentence — as many times as you want. Watch the passive: be + past participle, and the right preposition (by for the doer, from/in for the place).

"Our products are before distribution."
"Every batch is by our quality control team."
"The formula was last year."
"Regulations are set the health authority."
"Our raw materials are sourced vendors in Asia."
"No product is without proper certification."

Stage 2: The Company, in Order

Order

The path a product takes through your company — from the raw material to the customer, in the order it really happens.

?Each batch is tested by the quality control team.
?Raw materials are sourced from trusted vendors.
?The certification is confirmed and compliance is checked.
?The products are manufactured at the plant in Guarulhos.
?The shipment is sent to the distributor in Europe.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to the sentence, say it out loud, and only then record. Watch the long words: regulatory is "REG-yu-la-tory", distributor is "dis-TRIB-yu-ter", and certification is "cer-ti-fi-CA-tion".

We specialize in specialty chemicals, and I run production and regulatory affairs.
Our products are tested before they are shipped.
Our raw materials are sourced from vendors in Asia and Europe.
All regulations are set by the health authority, and every one is followed.
The plant is certified to international standards.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer. Question 1 is the trap: after "be", the verb is never left bare.

A distributor asks how you make sure your products are safe. You want to sound professional. You say:
A "Our products are test before they are ship."
B "Every batch is tested before it is released, and the whole process is monitored."
C "We are testing our products, someone tests, it is tested by us always."
Someone at a New York dinner asks, "So, what do you do?" The best first answer is:
A "I'm a director at a chemical company — we specialize in specialty chemicals." (one clean field sentence)
B A ten-minute description of every step of the manufacturing process.
C "I work in chemicals." (and then nothing else)
You want to say the health authority makes the rules. In the passive, that is:
A "The regulations are set from the health authority."
B "The regulations are set by the health authority." (the doer matters, so we use "by")
C "The regulations set the health authority."
You want to say your materials come from Asia. The natural passive is:
A "The raw materials are sourced by Asia."
B "The raw materials are sourced from vendors in Asia." (from/in for a place)
C "The raw materials source in Asia."
The technical detail is getting heavy and your guest looks lost. The best rescue line is:
A Keep going with even more detail until they understand.
B "It's a fairly complex process, but basically we make the raw materials others can't make safely."
C Stop talking and hand them a brochure.

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer — speak for 90 seconds, with no script.

You are at the Lyon fair, and a potential European distributor stops at your booth and asks what your company does. Give a 90-second introduction: your field (one clean sentence), how your products are made and tested (use the passive voice — "are tested", "are sourced", "are set by"), your supply chain and certification, and one human sentence to make it simple. Close by offering to send your company profile. This is the exact moment you will live in November — so take your time, and do not read from a script.

Survival Card -- Lesson 10

1We specialize in specialty chemicals, and I run production and regulatory affairs.
2Our products are tested before they are shipped.
3Our raw materials are sourced from vendors in Asia and Europe.
4All regulations are set by the health authority, and every one is followed.
5It's a fairly complex process, but basically we make the raw materials others can't make safely.
Lesson 11 -- Pre-class

Navigating Airports and Flights -- Check-in to Boarding (Consolidation)

A consolidation night at the airport: no new rule, just the words of the gate and the confidence to use what you already know. Key words: gate agent, overhead bin, aisle seat, window seat, boarding group, priority boarding, standby, customs declaration, tarmac, jet bridge, hold up, transit visa. Grammar review: the first conditional for real airport problems -- "If the flight is delayed, I'll ask for a voucher." Present simple after if, will in the result -- plus request modals (could, should, need to) for asking clearly at the desk.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Take your time -- listen twice, then say it out loud. These are the twelve words that live between the check-in desk and the boarding gate.

Gate agent -- the airline employee at the departure gate who checks tickets and helps passengers
"The gate agent scanned my boarding pass and pointed me to the jet bridge."
Overhead bin -- the closed storage space above the seats for your carry-on bag
"There was no room left in the overhead bin, so I put my bag under the seat."
Aisle seat -- the seat next to the walkway, easy to stand up and stretch
"I always book an aisle seat so I can stretch my legs on a long flight."
Window seat -- the seat next to the window, good for the view and for sleeping
"She prefers a window seat so she can sleep against the wall."
Boarding group -- the number or letter that tells you when it is your turn to board
"They called boarding group three, so it was finally my turn."
Priority boarding -- the right to get on the plane early, before most passengers
"My card gives me priority boarding, so I never fight for bin space."
Standby -- waiting for an open seat on a full flight, without a confirmed one
"I missed my flight, so they put me on standby for the next one."
Customs declaration -- the form where you list what you are bringing into a country
"I filled out the customs declaration before we landed in New York."
Tarmac -- the paved area outside where planes park, taxi, and take off
"We sat on the tarmac for an hour before we finally took off."
Jet bridge -- the covered walkway that connects the gate to the airplane door
"We walked down the jet bridge straight onto the plane."
Hold up -- to delay or stop someone, often used as “we were held up”
"We were held up at customs because of a long line."
Transit visa -- a document that lets you pass through a country on your way to another
"For a long layover in China, you may need a transit visa."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with the correct definition. Watch the pair that confuses everyone: an aisle seat is next to the walkway, and a window seat is by the wall.

Gate agent
Overhead bin
Aisle seat
Window seat
Boarding group
Priority boarding
Standby
Customs declaration
Tarmac
Jet bridge
Hold up
Transit visa

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text slowly, twice, and then answer the questions.

Lucimara has done this trip many times, so she plans out loud. If her flight to Paris is delayed, she 'll ask the gate agent about her connection to Lyon. Notice the shape: present simple after if -- is, not will be -- and will in the result.

At the desk, she asks for what she wants, clearly and politely. "I'd like an aisle seat, please." If her carry-on is too big for the overhead bin, the gate agent will check it for free. If the flight is full, she 'll go on standby for the next one.

She listens for her boarding group, and she watches the board. If the gate changes, she 'll walk to the new one right away. When her group is called, she goes down the jet bridge with her boarding pass ready.

A year ago, a delay or a gate change would have frozen her. Now she has a plan for each one, in English. The rules are not new -- the first conditional, "could you," "should I" -- but the airport is where she finally uses them without stopping to translate.

1. "If the flight is delayed, I'll ask for a voucher." Which part is correct?
A Present simple in both parts.
B Present simple after if, and will in the result. That is the first conditional.
C Will in both parts.
2. Which sentence is correct?
A "If the flight will be delayed, I'll ask for a voucher."
B "If the flight is delayed, I'll ask for a voucher." The if part stays in the present.
C "If the flight is delayed, I ask for a voucher tomorrow."
3. Your carry-on is too big for the overhead bin. What is most likely to happen?
A You must leave the bag behind.
B The gate agent checks it for free, and you pick it up at baggage claim.
C You cannot board the flight.
4. The flight is full and you don't have a seat. What does the agent put you on?
A Priority boarding.
B Standby -- you wait for an open seat on that flight.
C A transit visa.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- First Conditional (Review)

GRAMMAR

You met the first conditional earlier in the program. Here it is again, for the airport: a real, likely future. Present simple after if, and will in the result.

PartFormExample
The conditionIf + present simpleIf the flight is delayed,
The resultwill / 'll + base verbI 'll ask for a voucher.
Either orderresult + if (no comma)I'll check the board if the gate changes.
The one ideaA real, likely future. Present simple after if, will in the result -- never will after if.

Watch out -- the classic mistake: putting will in the if part. It is "If the flight is delayed...", never "If the flight will be delayed...". The will belongs in the result only. To ask at the desk, add a modal: "Could you check my bag?", "Should I stay near the gate?", "I need to make my connection."

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence. Tap Listen to hear the whole sentence -- as many times as you want.

"If the flight delayed, I'll ask for a voucher."
"I'd like an , please."
"If my bag doesn't fit, the will check it."
"The flight is full, so they put me on ."
"We were at customs because of a long line."
"For a long layover, you may need a ."

Stage 2: At the Gate, in Order

Order

This is how you get from the check-in desk to your seat -- in the order that actually happens, from arriving to boarding.

?Ask for your seat: "I'd like an aisle seat near the front, please."
?Give your destination: "I'm flying to Lyon, with a layover in Paris."
?Board: when your group is called, go down the jet bridge with your boarding pass ready.
?Sort out the bag: "If it doesn't fit in the overhead bin, could you check it?"
?Watch the board: "If the gate changes, I'll walk over to the new one."

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to the sentence, say it out loud, and only then record. Today, watch the silent s in "aisle" (it sounds like "I'll"), and keeping the if part in the present.

I'd like an aisle seat, please.
If the flight is delayed, I'll ask for a voucher.
Could you check my bag if it doesn't fit in the overhead bin?
If I miss my connection, I'll ask them to rebook me.
Do I need a transit visa for the layover?

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer. Some options are grammatically wrong; others are just too blunt or too weak for the moment.

Your flight might be delayed and you're worried about your connection. You say:
A "If the flight will be delayed, I miss my connection?"
B "If the flight is delayed, will I miss my connection?"
C "The flight delayed, my connection lost."
You want the seat next to the walkway, not the window. You ask:
A "Could I have a window seat, please?"
B "Could I have an aisle seat, please?"
C "Could I have a boarding group, please?"
Your carry-on won't fit in the overhead bin. Which is best?
A "Take my bag. It doesn't fit."
B "My bag won't fit in the overhead bin -- could you check it for me?"
C "Where is the tarmac for my bag?"
You have a twelve-hour layover in Shanghai. What should you check before you fly?
A "Do I need a transit visa for a long layover?"
B "Do I need a boarding group for the layover?"
C "Do I need an overhead bin for the layover?"
The flight is full and you don't have a confirmed seat. The agent says:
A "You have priority boarding for this full flight."
B "I'll put you on standby -- if a seat opens up, I'll call you."
C "Please fill out a customs declaration for the seat."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer -- speak for about 90 seconds, with no script.

Imagine you are at the airport, checking in for your flight to Lyon with a layover in Paris. Narrate the whole thing out loud: give your destination, ask for an aisle seat, deal with your carry-on, and then plan for problems with the first conditional -- "If the flight is delayed, I'll...", "If I miss my connection, I'll...", "If the gate changes, I'll...". Use at least four of today's new words and at least three first-conditional sentences. Ask, don't demand. Take your time and do not read from a script.

Survival Card -- Lesson 11

1I'd like an aisle seat, please.
2Is this flight on time?
3If my flight is delayed, will I miss my connection?
4Could you check my bag if it doesn't fit in the overhead bin?
5Do I need a transit visa for the layover?
Lesson 12 -- Pre-class

Hotels and Accommodation -- Checking In and Making Requests Like a Pro

In Lesson 11 you got off the plane at the airport. Now you arrive at your hotel in New York — and something is always a little off: the name is misspelled, the room faces the street, the air conditioning is faulty. This is the lesson for September 2026, the night you walk up to the front desk and handle everything yourself. Key words: lobby, key card, vacancy, minibar, double occupancy, adjoining room, rollaway bed, faulty, to settle the bill, to extend a stay. Structure: the indirect question — the polite way to ask for anything ("Could you tell me where the elevator is." / "I was wondering if I could move rooms." / "Do you know if breakfast is included."). The rule that catches everybody: after the soft opener, the word order goes back to normal ("where the elevator is", never "where is the elevator").
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Take your time — listen twice, and say it out loud. These are the ten words you will use at a hotel front desk, from the lobby to the moment you settle the bill.

Lobby -- the large open area near the entrance of a hotel, with seating and the front desk
"She crossed the bright lobby and gave her name at the front desk."
Key card -- the flat plastic card that unlocks your hotel room door
"He slid a key card across the counter for room three-oh-four."
Vacancy -- a room that is free and available to book at the hotel
"Luckily the hotel had a vacancy on a higher, quieter floor."
Minibar -- the small fridge in a hotel room with drinks and snacks you can buy
"She noticed the minibar, the courtyard view, and the quiet."
Double occupancy -- a room booked and priced for two people sharing it
"The courtyard room was the same rate for double occupancy."
Adjoining room -- a room connected to the one next to it by an inside door
"She asked whether they could set up an adjoining room for her colleague."
Rollaway bed -- an extra folding bed the hotel brings up for one more guest
"For a third guest, the hotel can bring up a rollaway bed."
Faulty -- not working correctly; broken (for a device, like an air conditioner)
"The air conditioning was faulty: it hummed loudly and blew warm air."
To settle the bill -- to pay the final total for your whole stay, usually at check-out
"He moved everything onto a single bill she could settle when she left."
To extend a stay -- to stay for one or more extra nights beyond your original booking
"I was wondering if I could extend my stay by one night, until Sunday."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with the correct definition. Watch the pair that is easy to mix up: an adjoining room is connected by an inside door, while a rollaway bed is just an extra bed brought into your room.

Lobby
Key card
Vacancy
Minibar
Double occupancy
Adjoining room
Rollaway bed
Faulty
To settle the bill
To extend a stay

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text slowly, twice, and then answer the questions. Notice how every request is a polite indirect question.

Lucimara reaches the front desk in the lobby and gives her name. The clerk cannot find it. "Could you tell me if the reservation might be under a different spelling?" she asks. "It's Ito, I-T-O." He checks again, and there it is. He hands her a key card for a room on the first floor, facing the street.

She does not demand a better room. She asks. "I was wondering if it would be possible to move to a higher, quieter floor. Do you know whether anything is available facing the back?" The clerk finds a vacancy on the fourteenth floor at the same rate for double occupancy.

In the new room, the air conditioning is faulty. She calls the desk. "I'm sorry to bother you, but the AC seems to be broken. Would you mind sending someone up, and could you tell me how long it might take?" An engineer arrives in fifteen minutes.

On the last morning she has one more request. "Could you tell me if I could extend my stay by one night, and settle the bill when I leave on Sunday?" The clerk confirms the room is free and moves everything onto one bill. Two years ago, she would have pointed and hoped. This week she asked, politely, and got everything she needed.

1. "Could you tell me where the elevator is?" Why is it "where the elevator is" and not "where is the elevator"?
A Both are the same; the order never changes.
B After a soft opener, the word order goes back to normal — subject ("the elevator") before verb ("is"), with no inversion.
C Because "elevator" is a plural word.
2. "Do you know if breakfast is included?" Why is "if" there?
A Because every polite question needs "if".
B Because it is a yes/no question — a yes/no question takes "if" or "whether" after the opener.
C Because "if" means the same as "where".
3. Why does "I was wondering if I could move rooms" sound more polite than "Give me another room"?
A The soft opener turns an order into a favor, so the other person wants to help.
B It is longer, and longer is always better.
C They mean exactly the same thing and sound the same.
4. A vacancy and an adjoining room — what is the difference?
A They are two words for the same thing.
B A vacancy is any free, available room; an adjoining room is one connected to yours by an inside door.
C A vacancy is on the first floor and an adjoining room is on a higher floor.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Indirect Questions (the polite way to ask)

GRAMMAR

Put a soft opener in front of your question, then say the rest as a calm, normal sentence. The opener does all the polite work.

PartFormExample
The soft openerCould you tell me / Do you know / I was wondering if / Would it be possible to"Could you tell me…"
Wh- questionopener + where/when/how + normal order"Could you tell me where the elevator is?"
Yes / No questionopener + if / whether + normal order"Do you know if breakfast is included?"
A requestI was wondering if I could… / Would it be possible to + base verb"I was wondering if I could move rooms."
The one ruleAfter the opener, drop the inversion: the verb comes AFTER the subject, exactly like a normal sentence. "Where is it?" → "…where it is." "Is it ready?" → "…if it is ready."

Watch out -- the classic mistakes: (1) keeping the inversion — "Could you tell me where is the elevator" should be "where the elevator is". (2) forgetting "if"/"whether" in a yes/no question — "Do you know breakfast is included" should be "Do you know if breakfast is included".

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each polite question. Tap Listen to hear the whole sentence — as many times as you want. Watch the word order after the opener, and the "if"/"whether" in yes/no questions.

"Could you tell me where the elevator ?"
"Do you know breakfast is included?"
"I was if I could move to a quieter room."
"Could you tell me what time check-out ?"
"Would it be possible get a late check-out?"
"I'm sorry to bother you, but the air conditioning is ."

Stage 2: The Arrival, in Order

Order

The steps of a hotel arrival — from walking in to leaving, in the order they really happen.

?The clerk hands you a key card for your room.
?You walk into the lobby and go to the front desk.
?You find the AC is faulty and call the desk to report it.
?You give your name and fix the spelling of the reservation.
?On the last day, you settle the bill and check out.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to the sentence, say it out loud, and only then record. Watch the tricky words: faulty is "FAWL-tee", occupancy is "OCC-u-pan-cy", and keep "I was wondering if" smooth, as one line.

Could you tell me where the elevator is?
I was wondering if it would be possible to move to a quieter room.
Do you know if breakfast is included in the rate?
I'm sorry to bother you, but the air conditioning is faulty.
Could you tell me if I could extend my stay and settle the bill on Sunday?

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer. Question 1 is the trap: after the opener, never keep the question word order.

You want to ask, politely, where the gym is. You say:
A "Could you tell me where is the gym?"
B "Could you tell me where the gym is?" (normal order after the opener)
C "Where is gym, please tell me now?"
Your room faces a noisy street. The most natural, polite request is:
A "I was wondering if it would be possible to move to a higher, quieter floor."
B "Give me another room, this one is bad."
C "I want quiet room now."
You want to check whether breakfast is included. The correct indirect question is:
A "Do you know is breakfast included?"
B "Do you know if breakfast is included?" (yes/no → if)
C "Do you know breakfast included?"
The air conditioning does not work. The best way to report it is:
A "Your air conditioning is broken, fix it."
B "I'm sorry to bother you, but the air conditioning seems to be faulty. Would you mind sending someone up?"
C Say nothing and just open the window.
It's your last morning and you want one more night. You say:
A "Could you tell me if I could extend my stay by one night and settle the bill on Sunday?"
B "I stay more. You change it."
C "Could you tell me if can I extend my stay?"

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer — speak for 90 seconds, with no script.

You have just arrived at your hotel in New York. Walk yourself through the whole check-in, out loud: greet the clerk, fix the misspelled name, ask to move to a quieter room, report the faulty air conditioning, and ask to extend your stay and settle the bill on Sunday. Make every request a polite indirect question ("Could you tell me if…", "I was wondering if…", "Would it be possible to…"). This is the exact night you will live in September — so take your time, and do not read from a script.

Survival Card -- Lesson 12

1It's Ito, I-T-O. Could you tell me if there's a reservation under that name?
2I was wondering if it would be possible to move to a higher, quieter floor.
3I'm sorry to bother you, but the air conditioning seems to be faulty.
4Do you know if breakfast is included in the rate?
5Could you tell me if I could extend my stay and settle the bill on Sunday?
Lesson 13 -- Pre-class

Asking for Directions -- Finding Your Way Around the City on Foot

From the hotel door to the museum, on foot: how to ask for and understand walking directions in New York. Key words: intersection, crosswalk, landmark, sidewalk, pedestrian, traffic light, around the corner, to head. Grammar: prepositions of place and movement -- place (across from, next to, on the corner of) tells you where a landmark is; movement (along, past, through, toward) tells you how to walk to it. A clear route uses both.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Take your time -- listen twice, then say it out loud. These are the eight words that live on the street, between your hotel door and the museum.

Intersection -- the place where two streets cross each other
"Turn left at the intersection with 53rd Street."
Crosswalk -- the marked white lines where people cross the street on foot
"Wait for the light and cross at the crosswalk."
Landmark -- a well-known building or place you use to find your way
"The tall glass tower is a good landmark to look for."
Sidewalk -- the paved path beside the street where people walk (the L is silent)
"Stay on the sidewalk and watch for cars."
Pedestrian -- a person walking in the street, not in a car
"The drivers here do not always wait for pedestrians."
Traffic light -- the red-yellow-green signal that tells cars when to stop and go
"Cross when the traffic light turns red for the cars."
Around the corner -- very close, just past the corner of the street
"The museum is around the corner, on your right."
To head -- to go or move in a certain direction (head north, head toward the park)
"From the door, head north on Fifth Avenue."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with the correct definition. Watch the pair that confuses everyone: a crosswalk is the place you cross, and a sidewalk is the path you walk along.

Intersection
Crosswalk
Landmark
Sidewalk
Pedestrian
Traffic light
Around the corner
To head

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text slowly, twice, and then answer the questions.

Lucimara's hotel is on Fifth Avenue, and the Museum of Modern Art is only two blocks away, so she decides to walk. She leaves the hotel and heads north along the avenue, staying on the sidewalk.

At the intersection with 53rd Street, she waits for the traffic light and crosses at the crosswalk with the other pedestrians. The museum is around the corner, on her right, across from a small park.

Notice the little words that do all the work. Some tell you where something is: across from the park, next to the bookstore, on the corner. Others tell you how you move: along the avenue, past the church, toward the river. These are prepositions of place and movement.

A year ago, Lucimara would have shown the address on her phone and hoped. Now she can ask for directions, understand the answer, and even give them back -- because a route is only clear when the little words are right.

1. "The museum is across from a small park." What does across from tell you?
A how you move to the museum
B where the museum is -- opposite the park
C when the museum opens
2. Which sentence uses the prepositions correctly?
A The cafe is in the corner, across the park.
B The cafe is on the corner, across from the park.
C The cafe is at corner, across of the park.
3. Where should pedestrians cross the street?
A In the middle of the block, wherever it is quick.
B At the crosswalk, when the traffic light lets them.
C On the sidewalk, without stopping.
4. "It is around the corner." What does this mean?
A Very close -- just past the corner of the street.
B Very far -- on the other side of the city.
C Behind the museum, underground.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Prepositions of Place and Movement

GRAMMAR

A direction is only clear because of small words. Place prepositions tell you where a landmark is; movement prepositions tell you how to walk to it. Use both, and any route becomes easy to follow.

TypeWordsExample
Place -- where it isacross from, next to, opposite, on the corner of, betweenThe museum is across from the park.
Movement -- how you goalong, past, through, toward, up / down, aroundWalk along Fifth, past the church.
The one ideaPlace tells you where a landmark is; movement tells you how to walk to it. A clear direction uses both.

Watch out -- the classic mistakes: it is "on Fifth Avenue", never "in Fifth Avenue"; you walk to the corner, never "walk until the corner"; and you cross at the crosswalk, not "in the crosswalk". And across means over: to say opposite, use across from.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence. Tap Listen to hear the whole sentence -- as many times as you want.

"Wait for the , then cross the street."
"Cross the street at the , not in the middle of the road."
"The museum is , just past the bank."
"Stay on the and watch for cars."
"Turn left at the with 53rd Street."
"Look for a like the tall glass tower."

Stage 2: The Route, in Order

Order

This is how you walk from the hotel to the museum -- in the order it actually happens, from the door to the front steps.

?The museum is around the corner, on your right, across from the park.
?Leave the hotel and head north along Fifth Avenue, on the sidewalk.
?Walk straight, past the church, for two blocks.
?Cross at the crosswalk and turn left onto 53rd Street.
?At the intersection with 53rd Street, wait for the traffic light.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to the sentence, say it out loud, and only then record. Today, watch the silent L in "sidewalk" and "crosswalk" (they sound like "wawk"), and the polite opener "Could you tell me...?"

Excuse me, could you tell me how to get to the museum?
Head north along Fifth Avenue and cross at the crosswalk.
The museum is around the corner, across from the park.
Stay on the sidewalk and watch for the traffic light.
Walk past the bakery and turn left at the intersection.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer. Some options are grammatically wrong; others are just not what a person would really say.

You are lost and want to ask a stranger the way to the museum. You say:
A Where is the museum? Tell me now.
B Excuse me, could you tell me how to get to the museum?
C Museum. You know? Where?
A tourist asks where the station is. It is opposite you, across the street. You say:
A It is across from us -- just cross at the crosswalk.
B It is in front of the corner, across the road inside.
C It is until the corner, on the middle.
You want to tell someone the museum is very close. You say:
A It is around the corner, next to the park.
B It is around the corner, next of the park.
C It is on the corner around, aside the park.
You want to say which way to walk on Fifth Avenue. Which is correct?
A Walk in Fifth Avenue until the light.
B Head north along Fifth Avenue to the light.
C Go by Fifth Avenue on the light front.
A pedestrian should cross a busy avenue:
A anywhere, quickly, between the cars.
B at the crosswalk, when the traffic light lets them.
C on the sidewalk, without looking.

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer -- speak for about 90 seconds, with no script.

Imagine a tourist stops you outside your hotel on Fifth Avenue and asks how to walk to the Museum of Modern Art. Give the whole route out loud: which way to head, where to cross, which street to turn on, the landmark to look for, and how far it is. Use at least four of today's new words and at least three prepositions -- a mix of place (across from, next to, on the corner) and movement (along, past, toward). Ask nothing; this time, you are the one who knows the way. Take your time and do not read from a script.

Survival Card -- Lesson 13

1Excuse me, could you tell me how to get to the museum?
2Is it far, or can I walk there?
3Should I cross at the crosswalk?
4Is there a landmark I can look for?
5So it is around the corner, across from the park -- thank you!
Lesson 14 -- Pre-class

Getting Around the City

How to compare your transport options in a big city and recommend the smartest way across town. Key words: toll, gridlock, shortcut, roundabout, one-way street, ferry, timetable, crowded, convenient, reliable, scenic, to drop off. Structure: comparatives (-er / more + than) and superlatives (the -est / the most).
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Toll -- a fee you pay to use a road, bridge, or tunnel
"The toll on the tunnel is higher than the bridge."
Gridlock -- a traffic jam so bad that cars cannot move
"There is heavy gridlock near the bridges."
Shortcut -- a quicker route than the usual one
"This alley is a shortcut to the station."
Roundabout -- a circular junction where traffic flows around a center
"Take the second exit at the roundabout."
One-way street -- a street where cars may drive in only one direction
"You cannot turn here; it is a one-way street."
Ferry -- a boat that carries people across water on a set route
"The ferry is the most scenic way to cross the river."
Timetable -- a schedule showing when buses or trains run
"Check the timetable before you leave."
Crowded -- full of people, with very little space
"The subway is crowded at rush hour."
Convenient -- easy to use and saving you time or effort
"A rideshare is more convenient with luggage."
Reliable -- working well and unlikely to fail or be late
"The subway is the most reliable option."
Scenic -- offering beautiful views along the way
"Take the scenic route by the water."
To drop off -- to take someone to a place and leave them there
"Can you drop me off at the corner?"

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with the correct definition.

Toll
Gridlock
Shortcut
Roundabout
One-way street
Ferry
Timetable
Crowded
Convenient
Reliable
Scenic
To drop off

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Lucimara needs to cross New York at rush hour. The roads are more crowded than the tracks, and there is heavy gridlock near the bridges, so the subway is faster than a taxi. The express is quicker than the local, and it is the most reliable ride at this hour. On the way back she wants a nicer view, so she takes the ferry: it is slower, but it is the most scenic route and less crowded than the trains. If she drives later, the bridge is cheaper than the tunnel, because the toll is lower this week.

1. 'The subway is faster than a taxi' compares how many options, and which form is it?
A Two options -- a comparative (-er + than).
B Three options -- a superlative.
C One option -- no comparison.
2. Why does the text say 'the most reliable' and not 'more reliable'?
A Because it compares only two trains.
B Because it picks the top ride out of several options.
C Because 'reliable' is a short word.
3. Which sentence is correct?
A A taxi is more expensive than the train.
B A taxi is expensiver than the train.
C A taxi is more expensiver than the train.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Comparatives & Superlatives

GRAMMAR

How to compare your options clearly.

FormUseExample
Comparative -- short wordadd -er + than: compare two thingsThe subway is faster than the bus.
Comparative -- long wordmore / less + word + than: compare two thingsA taxi is more expensive than the train.
Superlativethe + -est, or the most / least: top of three or moreThe ferry is the most scenic. Walking is the cheapest.
Irregularlearn these two by heartgood → better → the best; bad → worse → the worst.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the whole sentence.

"The subway is than the bus."
"A taxi is more than the train."
"The ferry is the most way to travel."
"Walking is the option of all."
"At rush hour the roads are more than the tracks."
"There is heavy near the bridges."

Stage 2: Put the Steps in Order

Order

Put the steps for choosing the smartest route in the correct order.

?Check the time and the traffic right now.
?Compare two options with a comparative (faster than, more crowded than).
?Decide where you are going and when.
?List the possible ways: subway, taxi, walking, ferry.
?Recommend the top choice with a superlative (the fastest, the most scenic).

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it.

The subway is faster than the bus.
A taxi is more expensive than the train.
The ferry is the most scenic route.
Walking is the cheapest option of all.
Could you drop me off at the corner, please?

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each real travel situation.

At rush hour, you compare the subway and a taxi. You say:
A The subway is faster than a taxi.
B The subway is more fast than a taxi.
C The subway is the faster than a taxi.
You recommend the top option out of several. You say:
A The ferry is the most scenic way back.
B The ferry is most scenic way back.
C The ferry is more scenic way back.
You compare the price of walking and a taxi. You say:
A Walking is cheaper than a taxi.
B Walking is cheapest than a taxi.
C Walking is more cheap than a taxi.
You describe the best ride using an irregular form. You say:
A The express is the best way to travel south.
B The express is the most good way to travel south.
C The express is the goodest way to travel south.

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine a friend is visiting your city for the first time and asks how to get around. Record a one-minute answer. Compare at least two ways to travel with a comparative ('the subway is faster than...'), pick your favorite with a superlative ('the best way is...'), and give one tip. Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 14

1What is the fastest way to get to Central Park?
2The subway is faster than a taxi at rush hour.
3Could you drop me off at the corner, please?
4The ferry is the most scenic route back.
5Which do you think is the best way across town?
Lesson 15 -- Pre-class

Small Talk That Works -- Starting, Sustaining, and Exiting Casual Conversations

The cocktail reception at the trade fair in Lyon: how to walk up to a stranger, open with an icebreaker, find common ground, keep the conversation alive, and exit gracefully. Key words: small talk, icebreaker, common ground, networking, reception, to mingle, follow-up question, to wrap up. Grammar: relative pronouns (who, which, that, where) -- the small words that connect two ideas into one smooth sentence: a company that makes ingredients, the colleague who introduced me, a city where the food is great.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Take your time -- listen twice, then say it out loud. These are the eight words that live in the room, between the first hello and the last goodbye.

Small talk -- light, friendly conversation about easy, everyday topics
"We made small talk about the weather and the food."
Icebreaker -- an easy opening line or question that starts a conversation
"A simple icebreaker like What brings you here always works."
Common ground -- a shared interest or experience that connects two people
"We found common ground when we saw we both work in cosmetics."
Networking -- meeting new people to build useful professional connections
"The reception is really about networking, not selling."
Reception -- a social event where guests gather, usually with drinks and food
"The reception starts at seven in the main hall."
To mingle -- to move around a room and talk with many different people
"After a few minutes, I moved on to mingle with other guests."
Follow-up question -- a question that digs deeper and keeps a conversation going
"Where in Lyon? is a good follow-up question."
To wrap up -- to bring a conversation, meeting, or talk to a polite end
"It was great meeting you is a warm way to wrap up."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with the correct definition. Watch the pair that confuses everyone: an icebreaker is how you START a conversation, and a follow-up question is how you KEEP it going.

Small talk
Icebreaker
Common ground
Networking
Reception
To mingle
Follow-up question
To wrap up

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text slowly, twice, and then answer the questions.

Tonight is the welcome reception at the trade fair in Lyon, and Lucimara knows almost no one. She takes a breath and walks up to a man who is standing alone. Her icebreaker is simple: "Hi, I don't think we've met. What brings you here?"

In a minute they find common ground. He is a buyer who sources ingredients; she runs a company that makes the very ingredients which go into his products. She keeps the small talk going with a follow-up question: "Lyon is a city where the food is amazing -- have you tried it yet?"

Notice the little words that do all the work: who for a person, that or which for a thing or a company, and where for a place. Each one glues a second idea onto a noun, so she says it all in one smooth line instead of two short ones.

When it is time to move on, she wraps up warmly: "It was great meeting you -- let's stay in touch." Then she goes off to mingle. A year ago she would have stayed silent in the corner; tonight she is doing real networking, in English.

1. "I run a company that makes ingredients." Why that and not who?
A because a company is a place
B because a company is a thing, not a person -- use that or which
C because the sentence is a question
2. Which sentence uses the relative pronoun correctly?
A He's the buyer which I met last year.
B He's the buyer who I met last year.
C He's the buyer what I met last year.
3. What is a good icebreaker at a reception?
A "Do you want to buy something from me?"
B "Hi, I don't think we've met. What brings you here?"
C "Why are you standing here alone?"
4. "Lyon is a city where the food is amazing." What does where connect?
A a place (the city) to more information about it
B a person to their job
C two questions in a row

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Relative Pronouns (who, which, that, where)

GRAMMAR

A relative pronoun glues a second idea onto a noun, so you can say it all in one smooth sentence. Choose the pronoun by what comes right before it: a person, a thing, or a place.

PronounUse it forExample
whopeoplethe colleague who introduced me
which / thatthings, companiesa company that makes ingredients
whereplacesa city where the food is great
The one ideaThe pronoun links a noun to more information about it — one smooth line instead of two short, choppy ones.

Watch out -- the classic mistakes: in Portuguese one word (que) does everything, but in English you choose. For a person, use who, never "which". For a thing or a company, use that or which, never "what". For a place, use where, not "that". And do not repeat the subject: the buyer who he sat next to me should be the buyer who sat next to me.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence. Tap Listen to hear the whole sentence -- as many times as you want.

"I work for a company makes chemical ingredients."
"That's the colleague introduced me to our supplier."
"Lyon is a city the food is incredible."
"A good is What brings you here?"
"We found because we both work in cosmetics."
"It was great meeting you is a warm way to ."

Stage 2: The Four Moves, in Order

Order

This is how a good small talk conversation flows -- in the order it actually happens, from the first hello to the goodbye.

?Wrap up warmly: "It was great meeting you -- let's stay in touch."
?Break the ice: "Hi, I don't think we've met. What brings you here?"
?Keep it going with a follow-up question: "Have you been to Lyon before?"
?Move on and mingle with other guests.
?Find common ground: "I run a company that makes ingredients like yours."

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to the sentence, say it out loud, and only then record. Today, watch the stress in "reception" (ri-SEP-shun) and the relaxed, natural rhythm of a real icebreaker.

Hi, I don't think we've met. What brings you here?
I work for a company that makes ingredients for personal care.
That's the colleague who introduced me to our supplier.
Lyon is a city where the food is incredible.
It was great meeting you -- let's stay in touch.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer. Some options are grammatically wrong; others are just not what a person would really say.

You walk up to a stranger at the reception. You say:
A Who are you? Tell me your job.
B Hi, I don't think we've met. What brings you here?
C You. Talk. Now, please.
You want to describe your company in one line. Which is correct?
A I work for a company that makes ingredients for cosmetics.
B I work for a company what makes ingredients for cosmetics.
C I work for a company who makes ingredients for cosmetics.
You want to introduce the colleague who helped you. You say:
A That's the colleague who introduced me to our supplier.
B That's the colleague which introduced me to our supplier.
C That's the colleague where introduced me to our supplier.
The conversation is ending and you want to leave politely. You say:
A Okay, I go now. Bye.
B It was great meeting you -- let's stay in touch.
C This conversation is finished for me.
You want to keep the conversation going. The best move is to:
A stay silent and wait for the other person.
B ask a follow-up question about what they just said.
C change the subject to something completely new.

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer -- speak for about 90 seconds, with no script.

Imagine you have just arrived at the welcome reception at the Lyon trade fair, and you walk up to a stranger. Out loud, carry the whole conversation: break the ice with an icebreaker, introduce yourself and your company in one line, find common ground, ask a follow-up question, and then wrap up gracefully. Use at least four of today's new words and at least three relative pronouns -- who for a person, that or which for your company, and where for a city you love. Take your time and do not read from a script.

Survival Card -- Lesson 15

1Hi, I don't think we've met. What brings you here?
2I work for a company that makes ingredients for personal care.
3Have you been to this fair before?
4Lyon is a city where the food is incredible.
5It was great meeting you -- let's stay in touch.
Lesson 16 -- Pre-class

Getting Around Like a Local

How different American cities move -- and how to report the travel advice a local gives you. Key words: walkable, grid system, urban sprawl, to carpool, surge pricing, bike lane, to reroute, bottleneck, crosstown, on foot, toll booth, to flag down. Structure: reported speech (said / told + verb one step into the past).
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Walkable -- easy to get around on foot
"She said the downtown was walkable, so I left the car behind."
Grid system -- streets laid out in a regular pattern of numbered blocks
"He told me the streets followed a grid system, so they were easy to find."
Urban sprawl -- the spread of a city outward over a large area
"A friend said the urban sprawl went on for miles."
To carpool -- to share a car ride with other people going the same way
"She said that most people carpooled to work."
Surge pricing -- higher rideshare prices when a lot of people want a ride
"He warned me that the app used surge pricing at rush hour."
Bike lane -- a marked part of the road only for bicycles
"She told me there was a bike lane along the river."
To reroute -- to change a planned route to avoid a problem
"He said the app would reroute me around the traffic."
Bottleneck -- a narrow point where traffic slows down and backs up
"She said a rideshare would only trap me in a bottleneck."
Crosstown -- going from one side of the city to the other
"He told me the subway was best for a crosstown trip."
On foot -- walking instead of using a car or transport
"She said that in some cities you move on foot."
Toll booth -- a place on a road or bridge where you stop to pay
"He said we would stop at a toll booth on the bridge."
To flag down -- to signal a taxi or bus to stop for you
"She told me to flag down a cab on the avenue."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with the correct definition.

Walkable
Grid system
Urban sprawl
To carpool
Surge pricing
Bike lane
To reroute
Bottleneck
Crosstown
On foot
Toll booth
To flag down

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Before her trip, Lucimara asked her friends how to get around. Her New York friend said that the city was walkable, so she could walk or take the subway. Her friend in Los Angeles told her that she would need a car, because the urban sprawl went on for miles. The app, he said, raised prices with surge pricing at rush hour. In the end, one friend told her that the smartest travelers always ask a local first.

1. In "She said that the city was walkable", why is the verb "was" and not "is"?
A In reported speech the verb moves one step into the past.
B Because the city is not walkable anymore.
C Because "say" is always followed by "was".
2. The text uses "told her that" and "said that". What is the difference?
A They mean exactly the same and follow the same pattern.
B "tell" needs a person (told her); "say" does not (said that).
C "say" needs a person; "tell" does not.
3. The direct words were "You will need a car." Which report is correct?
A He told her that she would need a car.
B He told that she will need a car.
C He said her that she would need a car.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Reported Speech

GRAMMAR

How to report what someone said to you.

Direct speechReported speechChange
"I live here."She said that she lived there.present → past
"You will need a car."He told me that I would need a car.will → would
"You can walk."She said that I could walk.can → could
say / tellsaid (that) · told me (that)tell needs a person; say does not

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the whole sentence.

"She said that the city was downtown."
"He told that I would need a car."
"She said that she in New York."
"He told me that I love the ferry."
"The app raised prices with pricing at rush hour."
"She said that I walk everywhere downtown."

Stage 2: Put the Steps in Order

Order

Put the steps for reporting someone's advice in the correct order.

?Choose your reporting verb: say (no person) or tell (a person).
?Report it to someone else in a full sentence.
?Listen carefully to what the person tells you.
?Move the verb one step into the past.
?Notice the exact words: is it a fact, a 'will', or a 'can'?

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it.

She told me that the city was walkable.
He said that I would need a car.
My friend explained that the streets were numbered.
They told me that prices went up at rush hour.
She said that I could walk everywhere downtown.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best way to report what each person said.

Your friend said: "I live in New York." You report:
A She said that she lived in New York.
B She said that she live in New York.
C She said me that she lived in New York.
A local said: "You will need a car." You report:
A He told me that I would need a car.
B He told that I would need a car.
C He said me that I will need a car.
The app said: "Prices are high now." You report:
A It said that prices were high then.
B It said that prices are high now.
C It told that prices were high.
Which sentence is correct?
A She told me that she could drive.
B She said me that she could drive.
C She told that she could drive.

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Think of three tips a friend once gave you about getting around a city. Record a one-minute answer reporting what they said. Use "She told me that..." and "He said that..." at least once each, and move the verbs one step into the past. Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 16

1She told me that the city was walkable.
2He said that I would need a car.
3My friend told me that the subway ran all night.
4They said that prices went up at rush hour.
5A local told me that I could walk everywhere.
Lesson 17 -- Pre-class

Checking In, Speaking Up -- Hotels, Reservations, and Handling Problems Confidently

The front desk of a New York hotel: your reservation is wrong, the Wi-Fi code fails, and the checkout time is off -- and you fix all three without raising your voice. Key words: to apologize, inconvenience, to accommodate, courtesy, to resolve, discrepancy, to escalate, reasonable.
