Grammar: modal verbs for polite requests and advice -- Could you upgrade my room?, Would it be possible to check in early?, You should speak to the manager. -- firm about the problem, kind to the person.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Take your time -- listen twice, then say it out loud. These are the eight words that live at a front desk, in the space between a problem and its solution.

To apologize -- to say you are sorry for a mistake or a problem
"The manager called to apologize for the mix-up with my room."
Inconvenience -- trouble or difficulty caused to someone
"We are sorry for the inconvenience, and we will fix it right away."
To accommodate -- to give a guest what they need or ask for
"Could you accommodate an early check-in, please?"
Courtesy -- polite behavior, or a kind gesture offered for free
"As a courtesy, the hotel added a free breakfast."
To resolve -- to find a solution to a problem
"I am sure we can resolve this here at the front desk."
Discrepancy -- a difference between two things that should match, like a bill
"There is a discrepancy on my bill -- a charge I do not recognize."
To escalate -- to take a problem to someone with more authority, like a manager
"If the clerk cannot help, I can escalate it to the duty manager."
Reasonable -- fair and sensible
"A free breakfast sounds like a reasonable way to fix it."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with the correct definition. Watch the pair that confuses everyone: to resolve is to fix the problem, and to escalate is to move it up to a manager.

To apologize
Inconvenience
To accommodate
Courtesy
To resolve
Discrepancy
To escalate
Reasonable

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text slowly, twice, and then answer the questions.

Lucimara arrives at the Harborview Hotel in New York after a long flight. She booked a superior room with an ocean view, but the clerk offers her a standard room on the ground floor. A year ago she would have accepted it in silence. Tonight she speaks up — firmly, and politely.

"There seems to be a discrepancy," she says. "I booked a superior room. Would it be possible to move me?" Notice the little word that does all the work: would. It turns a demand into a request the clerk wants to say yes to. She does not attack him; she names the problem and asks for the fix.

The clerk checks and finds she is right. "I apologize for the inconvenience," he says. "Could I offer you a complimentary breakfast as a courtesy?" That sounds reasonable, and she accepts. He resolves the room in two minutes.

If the clerk had not been able to help, she knew what to do: "You should ask for the manager," a friend once told her. When a problem is too big for the front desk, you escalate it — calmly, and with the same three small words: could, would, and should.

1. "Would it be possible to move me?" Why does she use would and not just "Move me"?
A because it is a question about the past
B because a modal turns a demand into a polite request people want to say yes to
C because "would" means she is unsure the room exists
2. Which sentence uses the modal correctly?
A You should to speak to the manager.
B You should speak to the manager.
C You should speaking to the manager.
3. What is the best way to name a hotel problem?
A "This is wrong! You gave me a terrible room!"
B "There seems to be a discrepancy -- I booked a superior room."
C "I do not like this. Give me another room now."
4. "You should ask for the manager." Is should a request or advice?
A advice — a recommendation about what to do
B a polite request for something you want
C a question about the future

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Modal Verbs (could, would, should)

GRAMMAR

A modal verb softens your words: you sound firm about the problem, but kind to the person. Use could and would for polite requests, and should for advice.

ModalUse it forExample
could you...?a polite request (softer than can)Could you help me, please?
would it be possible to...?a very polite requestWould it be possible to move me?
you should...advice (a recommendation)You should ask for the manager.
The one ideaThe modal makes you firm about the problem and kind to the person — and that is what gets a yes.

Watch out -- the classic mistakes: after a modal, use the base verb with no to — you should speak, never "you should to speak". Use only one modal at a time — not "Would you can help me?" but Could you help me?. And a modal never changes with he/she: she should ask, not "she shoulds ask".

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence. Tap Listen to hear the whole sentence -- as many times as you want.

"Excuse me, you upgrade my room, please?"
" it be possible to check in early?"
"If the room is wrong, you speak to the manager."
"There is a on my bill — a charge I never made."
"As a , they added a free breakfast."
"A free night sounds like a way to fix it."

Stage 2: The Four Moves, in Order

Order

This is how you handle a hotel problem well -- in the order it actually happens, from naming it to accepting the solution.

?If the clerk cannot help, escalate: "Could I speak with the duty manager, please?"
?Name the problem: "There seems to be a discrepancy -- I booked a superior room."
?Accept a fair solution: "A free breakfast sounds reasonable -- thank you."
?Request the fix politely: "Would it be possible to move me to the room I booked?"
?Confirm and move on: "Thank you for resolving it so quickly."

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to the sentence, say it out loud, and only then record. Today, keep the polite modal soft and connected -- "could you" almost becomes "couldja" in natural speech.

Could you upgrade my room, please?
Would it be possible to have a late checkout?
There seems to be a discrepancy on my bill.
You should speak to the manager about it.
Thank you for resolving it so quickly.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer. Some options are grammatically wrong; others are just not what a polite guest would really say.

You arrive and your room is not ready. You want to leave your bags. You say:
A Take my bags. I go now.
B Could you hold my bags for a little while, please?
C Would you can keep my bags here?
Your confirmation says superior, but you got a standard room. You say:
A There seems to be a discrepancy -- I booked a superior room. Would it be possible to move me?
B This room is wrong. Change it right now.
C Why you give me the bad room?
A shy friend has a bad room but says nothing. You give advice:
A You should to speak to the front desk.
B You should speak to the front desk -- they can't fix what they don't know about.
C You shoulds speak to the front desk.
There is a charge on your bill you did not make. You say:
A You are stealing my money with this charge.
B There's a charge I don't recognize -- would you mind checking it?
C This charge is wrong, remove now.
The clerk cannot help, and you need someone higher. You say:
A You are useless. Bring me a real person.
B I understand it's not your decision -- could I speak with the duty manager, please?
C I want the manager. Now.

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer -- speak for about 90 seconds, with no script.

Imagine you have just arrived at your hotel in New York. You booked a superior room with an ocean view, but the clerk offers you a standard room; the Wi-Fi code does not work; and there is a charge on your bill you do not recognize. Out loud, handle the whole check-in: give your name, name each problem calmly, request the fix with a modal (could, would), give one piece of advice with should, and accept the solution politely. Use at least four of today's new words and at least three modal verbs. Take your time and do not read from a script.

Survival Card -- Lesson 17

1I have a reservation under Ito -- could you check it, please?
2There seems to be a discrepancy -- I booked a superior room.
3Would it be possible to have a late checkout?
4There's a charge I don't recognize -- would you mind checking it?
5Could I speak with the duty manager, please?
Lesson 18 -- Pre-class

Ordering with Confidence

A New York restaurant with a business guest -- read the specials, ask about allergens, send back a dish, and split the check. Key words: server, daily special, house specialty, to eat out, to go with, to split the check, leftovers, on the house, allergen, dressing on the side, well-done, happy hour. Structure: the second conditional (if + past, would + base verb).
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Server -- the person who takes your order and brings your food
"The server told us about the daily specials before we ordered."
Daily special -- a dish the restaurant offers only that day, not on the regular menu
"If they had a fish daily special, I would definitely try it."
House specialty -- the dish a restaurant is most famous for
"She said the slow-cooked short rib was the house specialty."
To eat out -- to have a meal in a restaurant instead of at home
"When I travel, I love to eat out and try the local food."
To go with -- to choose something from the menu
"I think I will go with the grilled salmon."
To split the check -- to divide the bill so each person pays a share
"We are old friends, so we always split the check."
Leftovers -- the food you do not finish and take home to eat later
"The portion was huge, so I took the leftovers home."
On the house -- free, paid for by the restaurant
"The dessert was on the house because it was my birthday."
Allergen -- a food that can cause an allergic reaction, like nuts or shellfish
"I always ask about allergens because I cannot eat nuts."
Dressing on the side -- salad sauce served separately so you add your own amount
"Could I have the dressing on the side, please?"
Well-done -- cooked all the way through, with no pink inside
"He ordered his steak well-done."
Happy hour -- a time when drinks and snacks cost less
"If we arrived before seven, we would catch happy hour."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with the correct definition.

Server
Daily special
House specialty
To eat out
To go with
To split the check
Leftovers
On the house
Allergen
Dressing on the side
Well-done
Happy hour

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

When Lucimara imagines the perfect dinner, she uses the second conditional. If she were in New York tonight, she would eat out at a rooftop restaurant. If the server recommended the house specialty, she would order it. And if her guest had an allergy, the kitchen would prepare the dish separately. None of this is happening right now -- it is all imaginary, which is exactly what the second conditional is for.

1. In "If she were in New York, she would eat out", why is the verb "were"?
A The second conditional uses the past form, and "were" is used for all subjects.
B Because there were two or more people.
C Because "if" is always followed by "were".
2. The direct idea is "Maybe I will order the salmon (real)." How do you make it imaginary?
A If they have the salmon, I will order it.
B If they had the salmon, I would order it.
C If they would have the salmon, I will order it.
3. Which sentence is correct?
A If I were you, I would try the house specialty.
B If I would be you, I would try the house specialty.
C If I were you, I will try the house specialty.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Second Conditional

GRAMMAR

How to talk about imaginary situations.

SituationStructureExample
Real / likely (first)if + present, will + base"If they have it, I will order it."
Imaginary (second)if + past, would + base"If they had it, I would order it."
Giving adviceIf I were you, I would..."If I were you, I would try the special."
'were' for everybodyif I / he / she were"If she were here, she would love it."

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the whole sentence.

"If I you, I would order the house specialty."
"If they had it, I order it right away."
"If the server it, I would try it."
"Could I have the dressing the side, please?"
"We would like to the check, please."
"If we arrived before seven, we would catch happy ."

Stage 2: Put the Steps in Order

Order

Put the steps for ordering at an American restaurant in the correct order.

?Mention any allergen or change you need.
?At the end, ask to split the check.
?Read the menu and the daily specials.
?Say your choice: 'I'll go with...'.
?Ask the server what they would recommend.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it.

If I were you, I would go with the house specialty.
Could you tell us about the daily special?
Is there any allergen in this dish?
We would like to split the check, please.
If they had a vegetarian option, I would order it.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each situation at the restaurant.

A friend cannot decide what to order. You give advice:
A If I were you, I would go with the house specialty.
B If I am you, I will go with the house specialty.
C If I would be you, I would go with the house specialty.
You want your salad sauce served separately. You say:
A Could I have the dressing on the side, please?
B Could I have the dressing in the corner, please?
C Could I have the salad without the salad, please?
You and a friend want to divide the bill. You say:
A We would like to split the check, please.
B We would like to break the check, please.
C We would like to cut the money, please.
Imagine a hypothetical free dinner anywhere. Which sentence is correct?
A If I could eat anywhere, I would go to New York.
B If I can eat anywhere, I would go to New York.
C If I could eat anywhere, I will go to New York.

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine your ideal meal in New York. Record a one-minute answer: where you would go, what you would order, and who you would bring. Use the second conditional ("If I were... I would...") at least twice, and include five words from today. Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 18

1If I were you, I would go with the house specialty.
2Could I have the dressing on the side, please?
3Is there any allergen in this dish?
4We would like to split the check, please.
5Could we get a box for the leftovers?
Lesson 19 -- Pre-class

Small Talk That Works

Start and sustain casual conversations with native speakers. New expressions: what brings you here, I couldn't help but notice, to hit it off, small world, no kidding, that reminds me, speaking of, filler words. Grammar review (consolidation): soft modal verbs (might want to, could, should probably) and the second conditional (if I lived here, I'd...) in social contexts.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each expression and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

What brings you here? -- a light, warm way to ask why someone is in a place
"What brings you here today -- work or a little vacation?"
I couldn't help but notice -- a soft, polite way to start a comment about something you saw
"I couldn't help but notice your bag -- is it from that new shop?"
To hit it off -- to like someone and get along well, right from the start
"We started talking about coffee and just hit it off."
Small world! -- a warm reaction when you discover an unexpected connection
"You know my cousin? Small world!"
No kidding! -- a friendly reaction showing interest or mild surprise
"You lived in Tokyo too? No kidding!"
That reminds me -- a bridge to a new topic that the conversation just sparked
"That reminds me -- have you tried the bakery next door?"
Speaking of -- a smooth link to a topic that was just mentioned
"Speaking of coffee, do you know a good place near here?"
Filler words -- small words that give you time and signal you are engaged
"I was, you know, a little nervous, but it went well."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each expression with the correct meaning.

What brings you here?
I couldn't help but notice
To hit it off
Small world!
No kidding!
That reminds me
Speaking of
Filler words

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

At a cafe in Brooklyn, I saw a woman with a book from a store I love. I couldn't help but notice it, so I said hello. When she said she was from Sao Paulo, I said, no kidding -- my sister lived there. Small world! We hit it off right away. I told her she might want to try the cold brew, and that we could visit the bakery next door. Speaking of the bakery, that reminds me -- their croissants are amazing. I said, if I lived here, I'd come every morning. A few filler words here and there kept it easy. After ten minutes, I said I should probably get going, but it was great talking to her.

1. When the writer says "you might want to try the cold brew", is it an order or a soft suggestion?
A A soft, friendly suggestion.
B A strong order.
C A complaint.
2. In "if I lived here, I'd come every morning", does the writer live there now?
A Yes, she lives there now.
B No -- it is imaginary (second conditional).
C It is a real future plan.
3. Why does the writer use a few filler words?
A Because she made a mistake.
B To keep the conversation easy and show she was engaged.
C To end the conversation quickly.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Soft Modals & Second Conditional (Review)

GRAMMAR

Nothing new today -- just two tools you already have, used socially. Use soft modals (might want to, could, should probably) to suggest gently instead of giving orders. Use the second conditional (if + past, would/'d) to imagine a social what-if. Inside if, use the past -- never "would".

ToolSocial useExample
might want toa gentle suggestion"You might want to try that cafe."
coulda light, no-pressure offer"We could grab a coffee sometime."
should probablya soft intention"I should probably head out soon."
if + past, 'da social what-if (not real now)"If I lived here, I'd come every day."

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence. Tap Listen to hear the whole sentence.

"I couldn't help but your camera."
"You want to try the rooftop bar."
"If I here, I would walk everywhere."
"We grab a coffee sometime."
"You know my hometown? !"
"I probably head out, but this was nice."

Stage 2: Put the Steps in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of a small talk in the correct order.

?Listen closely and react warmly, like "no kidding" or "that is interesting".
?Notice a small, safe opening, like a bag, a jacket, or the weather.
?Close in a friendly way, saying "it was great talking to you".
?Open with a light comment or question, like "what brings you here".
?Keep it going with a follow-up, using "speaking of" or "that reminds me".

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

What brings you here today?
I couldn't help but notice your bag.
If I lived here, I'd come every day.
It was great talking to you.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each casual situation.

You want to give a light tip about a restaurant. What do you say?
A "You must eat at that place."
B "You might want to try that place."
C "Eat at that place now."
You want to imagine living in the city. What do you say?
A "If I would live here, I would walk everywhere."
B "If I lived here, I would walk everywhere."
C "If I live here, I will walk everywhere."
A stranger says they also love your hometown. How do you react?
A "No kidding! Small world."
B "That is none of my business."
C "Please stop talking."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine you are waiting in line at a coffee shop in New York. Start a small talk with the person next to you, and play both sides for about a minute. Use at least four new expressions, one soft modal, and one second conditional ("if I lived here, I'd..."). Open, react, keep it going, and close warmly.

Survival Card -- Lesson 19

1What brings you here today?
2I couldn't help but notice your bag.
3No kidding! Small world.
4That reminds me -- you might want to try that place.
5It was great talking to you.
Lesson 20 -- Pre-class

Understanding Real English

Decode fast American English in stores and markets: connected speech, reductions, and listening for keywords instead of every word. Key words: reduction, contraction, connected speech, intonation, word stress, inference, to get the gist, to tune in, to tune out, to catch on, to pick up, to lose the thread. Structure: "used to + infinitive" for past habits that changed.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Reduction -- a shortened, relaxed form of words in fast speech, like 'gonna' for 'going to'
"In fast speech, 'want to' becomes the reduction 'wanna'."
Contraction -- two words joined and shortened, like 'don't' or 'let's'
"'Do not' turns into the contraction 'don't' in normal speech."
Connected speech -- the way words blend together when people talk quickly
"In connected speech, 'what are you' can sound like 'whatcha'."
Intonation -- the rise and fall of the voice that adds meaning
"Her intonation went up, so I knew it was a question."
Word stress -- the syllable you say louder and longer in a word
"Word stress on the important words helps you catch the meaning."
Inference -- a smart guess about meaning based on clues and context
"I did not hear the price, but I made an inference from her gesture."
To get the gist -- to understand the general meaning without catching every word
"I did not catch every word, but I got the gist of the sale."
To tune in -- to start listening closely and follow what is said
"When the clerk spoke, I tried to tune in to the keywords."
To tune out -- to stop paying attention to a sound or a voice
"The store was noisy, so I had to tune out the background music."
To catch on -- to begin to understand something after a moment
"At first it was fast, but I started to catch on."
To pick up -- to learn words informally, just by hearing them
"I picked up a few phrases just by shopping in New York."
To lose the thread -- to stop following what someone is saying
"He spoke so fast that I lost the thread for a second."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with the correct definition.

Reduction
Contraction
Connected speech
Intonation
Word stress
Inference
To get the gist
To tune in
To tune out
To catch on
To pick up
To lose the thread

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Lucimara's listening has changed a lot. She used to freeze the moment someone spoke fast. She used to translate every single word, and she often lost the thread. She didn't use to notice reductions like "gonna" or "wanna". Now everything is different: she listens for keywords, she gets the gist, and she picks up new phrases just by hearing them. What used to feel impossible now feels normal.

1. In "She used to freeze", what does "used to" tell us?
A It was a past habit that is no longer true.
B It is something she does now.
C It will happen in the future.
2. Which sentence is correct?
A She didn't use to notice reductions.
B She didn't used to notice reductions.
C She doesn't used to notice reductions.
3. How do you turn "I used to translate every word" into a question?
A Did you use to translate every word?
B Did you used to translate every word?
C Used you to translate every word?

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Used To

GRAMMAR

How to talk about past habits that have changed.

FormStructureExample
Affirmativeused to + base verb"I used to freeze when people spoke fast."
Negativedidn't use to + base"I didn't use to understand reductions."
QuestionDid ... use to + base?"Did you use to translate every word?"
Meaningpast habit, no longer true"I used to tune out, but now I tune in."

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the whole sentence.

"I to freeze when people spoke fast."
"Did you to translate every word?"
"I listen for keywords to get the ."
"In fast speech, going to becomes the gonna."
"The store was loud, so I had to tune the noise."
"At first it was fast, but I started to catch ."

Stage 2: Put the Steps in Order

Order

Put the steps for understanding fast English in the correct order.

?Get the gist from the words you catch.
?Later, notice the reductions and pick them up.
?Relax and do not panic when the speech is fast.
?If you missed it, ask them to slow down or repeat.
?Tune in and listen for the keywords, not every word.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it.

I used to freeze, but now I listen for keywords.
Sorry, could you say that a little slower?
Do you have this in a smaller size?
I did not catch that. One more time, please?
I used to tune out fast speech, but now I tune in.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each situation.

A clerk speaks too fast and you miss part of it. You say:
A Sorry, could you say that a little slower?
B Please, speak the slow for me.
C Repeat again all the words now.
You want to describe a past habit that has changed. Which is correct?
A I used to freeze when people spoke fast.
B I use to freeze when people spoke fast.
C I am used to freeze when people spoke fast.
You only caught "sale" and "today". Getting the gist means:
A understanding the general idea without every word.
B translating each word carefully in your head.
C repeating the whole sentence back exactly.
Which question is correct?
A Did you use to translate every word?
B Did you used to translate every word?
C Do you used to translate every word?

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

How did your English listening used to be, and how is it now? Record a one-minute answer. Use "used to" at least twice, and include five words from today (for example: reduction, get the gist, tune in, catch on, pick up). Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 20

1Sorry, could you say that a little slower?
2I did not catch that. One more time, please?
3Do you have this in a smaller size?
4I used to freeze, but now I listen for keywords.
5I am just browsing, thanks.

IN CLASS -- Select your Lesson

01
Who Is Lucimara? -- Diagnostic Session and Personal Introduction
Introducing yourself and your work at an international trade fair in New York -- 35 slides
02
Arriving and Settling In -- Airports, Hotels, and the First Hours in a New City
Landing at JFK, going through customs and checking into a hotel in New York -- 38 slides
03
Getting Around and Getting Things Done -- Taxis, Subways, Restaurants, and Shops
A cidade em movimento: táxi, metrô, diner e loja na Fifth Avenue -- 37 slides
04
If I Miss My Flight -- Handling Problems, Contingencies, and Unexpected Situations
Voo cancelado em Miami: remarcar, pedir voucher e resolver a noite em inglês -- 38 slides
05
What Would You Do? -- Dream Trips, Hypotheticals, and the Week You Would Choose
Do problema real ao sonho real: second conditional, I wish e a semana perfeita em Nova York -- 37 slides
06
Getting Around Like a Local -- Airports, Transit, and the Rules of the City
Do quiosque de check-in em Guarulhos a catraca em Manhattan: have to, don't have to, can't e be allowed to -- a gramatica de uma regra -- 39 slides
07
Speaking Up with Confidence -- Hotels, Reservations, and When Things Go Wrong
Do check-in ao problema na conta: pedidos educados e perguntas indiretas -- como falar firme sem soar grosseira -- 36 slides
08
Eating Out with Confidence -- Restaurants, Orders, and Recommendations
Da reserva a conta em um restaurante de Nova York: como recomendar, sugerir e pedir -- I'd recommend, you might want to try, why don't you, should I -- 39 slides
09
Shopping, Returning, and Negotiating Price -- Being Direct and Polite in American Stores
Da devolucao de um casaco defeituoso a negociar preco: used to para habitos que mudaram, e como ser direta e educada no balcao -- exchange, return policy, price match, store credit -- 36 slides
10
Talking About Your Work -- Explaining Your Role and Industry with Confidence
Da feira de Lyon a um jantar em Nova York: como explicar sua empresa e sua industria em ingles simples -- passive voice (are tested, are set, was approved) e o vocabulario de negocios e regulatorio -- 39 slides
11
Navigating Airports and Flights -- Check-in to Boarding (Consolidation)
A consolidation of the first conditional and request modals, at the airport: check-in, seat choice, boarding, and a gate change on the way to Lyon -- gate agent, overhead bin, aisle seat, boarding group, standby, jet bridge -- 35 slides
12
Hotels and Accommodation -- Checking In and Making Requests Like a Pro
Da chegada em Nova York ao balcao do hotel: como fazer pedidos educados e resolver problemas com indirect questions ('Could you tell me...', 'I was wondering if...') e o vocabulario de hospedagem -- lobby, key card, minibar, adjoining room -- 39 slides
13
Asking for Directions -- Finding Your Way Around the City on Foot
From the hotel door to the museum, on foot: how to ask for and understand walking directions in New York -- cross at the crosswalk, head up Fifth Avenue, it's around the corner. New words for the street (intersection, crosswalk, landmark, sidewalk, pedestrian, traffic light) and the grammar that makes a route clear: prepositions of place and movement (across from, next to, past, along, toward) -- 35 slides
14
Getting Around the City
Compare your transport options in New York with comparatives and superlatives -- 36 slides
15
Small Talk That Works -- Starting, Sustaining, and Exiting Casual Conversations
The cocktail reception at the trade fair in Lyon: how to walk up to a stranger, open with an icebreaker, find common ground, keep it going with a follow-up question, and exit gracefully -- 'It was great meeting you, let\'s stay in touch.' New words for the room (small talk, icebreaker, common ground, networking, reception, to mingle, follow-up question, to wrap up) and the grammar that connects your ideas: relative pronouns (who, which, that, where) -- 35 slides
16
Getting Around Like a Local
Report the travel advice locals give you as you learn how different American cities move -- reported speech -- 36 slides
17
Checking In, Speaking Up -- Hotels, Reservations, and Handling Problems Confidently
The front desk of a New York hotel: your reservation is wrong, the Wi-Fi code fails, and the checkout time is off -- and you fix all three without raising your voice. The words of the desk (to apologize, inconvenience, to accommodate, courtesy, to resolve, discrepancy, to escalate, reasonable) and the grammar that makes you sound firm but polite: modal verbs for polite requests and advice -- 'Could you upgrade my room?', 'Would it be possible to check in early?', 'You should speak to the manager.' -- 35 slides
18
Ordering with Confidence
A New York restaurant with a business guest: read the daily specials, ask about allergens, send back a dish, and split the check -- all in natural American English. The words of the table (server, daily special, house specialty, to eat out, to go with, to split the check, leftovers, on the house, allergen, dressing on the side, well-done, happy hour) and the grammar that lets you imagine any meal: the second conditional -- 'If I were you, I would go with the house specialty', 'If the server recommended it, I would try it' -- contrasted with the first conditional. -- 36 slides
19
Small Talk That Works -- Modal & Conditional Consolidation
Start and sustain casual conversations with native speakers -- consolidating modal verbs and the second conditional in social contexts -- 33 slides
20
Understanding Real English
Decode fast American English in stores and markets: connected speech, reductions, and the art of listening for keywords instead of every word. Read a strategy guide, hear a fast clerk, and learn to slow the conversation down. The words of real listening (reduction, contraction, connected speech, intonation, word stress, inference, to get the gist, to tune in, to tune out, to catch on, to pick up, to lose the thread) and the grammar that tells your learning story: 'used to + infinitive' -- 'I used to freeze when people spoke fast, but now I listen for keywords.' -- 36 slides

Complementary Activities

Your 40 minutes to work and 40 minutes back are the biggest asset of this program. These materials were chosen for the car — audio first. Mark them as done after you listen.

Lesson 1 — Who Is Lucimara? -- Diagnostic Session and Personal Introduction

Podcast
American English Podcast — Ep. 208: How to Improve the Four Skills (Listening, Reading, Speaking and Writing)

This is the base podcast of your program: American English, controlled pace, full transcript on the website. This episode goes straight to your pain point — how to really train your ear, instead of just having English playing in the background.

Tip (do it in the car): listen ONCE, without pausing. Then write down 3 sentences you understood clearly and 2 you could NOT catch. Bring those 2 to lesson 2 — they become the mini listening drill of the next class.

Listen ↗
Podcast — current affairs
CNN 10 — the news of the day in 10 minutes

Ten minutes, American English, a firm but clear pace, and a new episode every weekday. It is the perfect format for the drive to Guarulhos: it fits the commute, and the topic is the news — the same kind of small talk that comes up at a trade fair dinner.

Tip: at the beginning, listen to the same episode TWICE (once on the way there, once on the way back). The second time you will catch whole sentences that you missed. That is not repetition for nothing — that is how the ear is trained.

Listen ↗
YouTube
English Tenses: Learn PRESENT PERFECT with a game (engVid)

The structure of lesson 1 — have / has + past participle with for and since. It is the sentence that opens any professional conversation of yours: “I’ve worked in the chemical industry for twenty years.” The lesson introduces it; this video makes it stick.

Tip: after the video, say 5 sentences about YOUR life — two with present perfect (for / since), two with present continuous (what you are doing this month) and one with present simple (your routine). Out loud, without writing them first.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 2 — Arriving and Settling In -- Airports, Hotels, and the First Hours in a New City

Podcast
American English Podcast — Ep. 111: Chats with Shana — A Vacation in Hawaii

Two American women telling the story of a real trip: the flight, the arrival, the hotel, what went well and what did not. This is exactly the language of Lesson 2 — the story of the arrival in the past simple (“we landed”, “we took a cab”) mixed with the present perfect of what has already happened (“we’ve already been there”). American English, natural speed, transcript on the website.

Tip (do it in the car, on the way there): listen once, all the way through, with no pauses. Then write down every time you hear just, already or yet. You do not need to understand the whole sentence — hunting for ONE word already trains your ear, and that word is our grammar this week.

Listen ↗
Podcast — a story in English
American English Podcast — Ep. 168: Irregular Verbs with the Statue of Liberty

A New York story told slowly, made to train exactly the past participles (been, gone, taken, seen) — the missing piece of the present perfect. Twenty minutes, American English, and the setting is the city where you land in September.

Tip (on the way back): listen and repeat out loud every participle that trips you up. Then build sentences about YOUR trip: “I’ve been to New York three times”, “I’ve never taken a cab from JFK at night”.

Listen ↗
YouTube
Already vs. Still vs. Yet — Speak English With Vanessa

The grammar of Lesson 2, in American English, with a teacher who speaks slowly and repeats: when to use already (it is done) and when to use yet (not up to now) — and why yet always goes at the end of the sentence.

Tip: after the video, say five sentences out loud about your trip in September — two with already (“I’ve already booked the hotel”), two with yet (“I haven’t bought the tickets yet”) and one with just. Do not write them down first.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 3 — Getting Around and Getting Things Done -- Taxis, Subways, Restaurants, and Shops

Podcast
American English Podcast — Tipping in the US

The tipping code explained by an American: how much you leave at a restaurant, in a taxi, at a hotel, and what happens when you leave nothing at all. This is lesson 3, word for word — tip, sales tax, receipt — and it answers, once and for all, the question that freezes you when the check arrives in New York.

Tip (do this in the car, on the way there): listen ONCE, all the way through, without pausing. Then write down EVERY number and EVERY percentage you caught — and 2 sentences you did NOT catch. Bring both to class: they become the mini listening lesson in lesson 4.

Listen ↗
Podcast — 5-Minute English
American English Podcast — Ep. 153: Shopping for Clothes

Five minutes with the exact language of the store: asking for another size, asking where the fitting room is, trying a coat on (try it on), and the price that goes up at the register because of the sales tax. American English, natural speed, and short enough to hear twice on one trip.

Tip (on the way back): listen twice. The second time, repeat OUT LOUD every question the host teaches — your mouth needs the repetition, not only your ears. Then build 3 sentences about the coat you are going to buy on Fifth Avenue in September.

Listen ↗
YouTube — conversation (you can listen without watching)
Present Perfect and the Past Simple — English Conversation about Experiences

A conversation in American English, about trips and life experiences, made for you to HEAR the difference between "I went to New York in 2023" (closed, with a date) and "I have never taken the subway" (a whole life, no date). It is the grammar of lesson 3 in the mouths of people talking, not on a board.

Tip: it works as pure audio — listen in the car, without looking at the screen. Every time you hear a verb after have / has, say the pair out loud: take → taken, go → gone, eat → eaten. That pair is what gives your level away when you speak.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 4 — If I Miss My Flight -- Handling Problems, Contingencies, and Unexpected Situations

Podcast — Expression
American English Podcast — Ep. 146: Up in the Air

One expression that is the whole of Lesson 4 in three words: up in the air = nothing is decided yet, everything can still change. It is exactly the state of your night when the board turns red — and it is what you say to a colleague back home when he asks what time you land: “Everything is still up in the air. If they rebook me tonight, I’ll land at six.” The host speaks slowly and repeats the expression in several contexts.

Tip (do this in the car, on the way there): listen once, all the way through, without pausing. Then build 3 sentences of your own with up in the air + the first conditional, about September: one about the flight, one about the meeting, one about the fair. Bring all three to Lesson 5.

Listen ↗
Podcast — Expression
American English Podcast — Ep. 117: Between a Rock and a Hard Place

The expression for the exact moment at the desk in Miami: two options, and both of them bad — sleep at the airport or miss the meeting. “I was between a rock and a hard place: unless I took the Charlotte flight, I’d miss the meeting.” An American uses this in a business meeting without thinking twice, and it opens a whole conversation about a hard decision — which is your home ground.

Tip (on the way back): listen twice. The second time, PAUSE every time you hear an if or an unless and repeat the whole sentence out loud. The mouth needs the repetition, not only the ear — and the first conditional will only come out automatically at the desk if it comes out automatically in the car.

Listen ↗
YouTube — grammar (with the mistakes you are going to make)
How To Use The FIRST Conditional (+ Common Mistakes To Avoid!) — English with Greg

The grammar of Lesson 4, explained in clear English and focused on the MISTAKES — including the two we saw in class: the will that slips inside the if, and the double negative after unless. It is worth it precisely because he does not stop at the rule: he shows the rule being BROKEN, which is how you will recognize your own mistake when it comes out of your mouth.

Tip: watch it once with English subtitles. Then close your eyes and listen again, audio only, repeating each example out loud. And every time he shows a mistake, PAUSE and say the correct version BEFORE he does — if you catch both mistakes from Lesson 4, slide 24 (Spot the Error) is already won.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 5 — What Would You Do? -- Dream Trips, Hypotheticals, and the Week You Would Choose

Podcast
American English Podcast — Ep. 106: A Dream Trip to the Maldives ("Off the Beaten Track")

This is the whole of Lesson 5, in natural American English: a conversation about the dream trip — the one that has not happened yet. You will hear the second conditional in the wild (“if I had the money, I would…”) and the expression you learned today: off the beaten path (in the British version, track — same idea).

Tip (do this in the car, on the way there): listen ONCE, all the way through, with no pauses. Then write down EVERY sentence that starts with if or has a would in it — and bring 2 that you could NOT put together on your own. They become the mini listening lesson in Lesson 6.

Listen ↗
Podcast — pure hypothetical
American English Podcast — Ep. 109: Dream Houses with Pete

Two Americans describing their dream house — in other words, forty minutes of “I would…”, “I’d rather…” and “if I could…”, with no blackboard anywhere. The topic is not travel, but the grammar is exactly yours: the world that does not exist yet. Notice how the would almost disappears in fast speech — it turns into a single d glued to the pronoun.

Tip (on the way back): listen, and every time an “I’d” goes by, repeat the whole sentence OUT LOUD. Then answer their question out loud, alone in the car: “If you could live anywhere in the world, where would you live?” — 60 seconds, without stopping.

Listen ↗
YouTube — conversation (you can listen without watching)
Native English Conversations — 2nd Conditional: "What would you do if you had $1m?"

Two native speakers, Matt and Mark, having a real conversation — no teacher, no whiteboard. They ask each other hypothetical questions and answer at real speed: “What would you do if…” / “I’d probably…”. It is today’s grammar in the mouth of people who are not teaching anything, which is exactly where you will meet it in September.

Tip: this works as pure audio — listen in the car, without looking at the screen. The rule of the exercise: pause every 3 or 4 sentences and sum up OUT LOUD, in one single sentence, what you have just heard. It is tiring for the first five minutes, and it is the listening practice that pays off the most.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 6 — Getting Around Like a Local -- Airports, Transit, and the Rules of the City

YouTube — tonight's grammar, in the wild
New York City Subway — Complete Guide, Do's and Don'ts (Tips from a Local)

A New Yorker explaining, one by one, the rules of the subway you are going to take in September. Look at the title: do's and don'ts is exactly tonight's grammar — what you have to do, what you can't do, and what you don't have to do. He speaks fast, and he swallows every single hafta.

Tip: watch it ONCE with English subtitles. The second time, close your eyes and only listen — and write down every RULE you catch, sorting it into obligation, permission or prohibition. Bring the three you find most useful for September.

Watch on YouTube ↗
Podcast — for the car (the drive to Guarulhos and back)
American English Podcast — Ep. 186.2: The Violinist in the Metro

The true story of the violinist who played, anonymous, in a subway station — and almost nobody stopped. It is natural American English, narrated, in the exact setting of tonight's lesson: the platform, the crowd, the rush. The goal here is not grammar: it is your ear. It is the gap you named yourself, and these are your eighty minutes a day in the car.

Tip (the exercise that pays the most): listen ONCE all the way through, without pausing, without understanding everything — and do not get annoyed about that. The second time, pause every 3 or 4 sentences and sum up OUT LOUD, in one sentence, what you have just heard. Bring 2 sentences you could NOT put together: they become the listening mini-lesson of Lesson 7.

Listen ↗
YouTube — the practical step by step (you can listen without watching)
How to Ride the NYC Subway — With Tips & Tricks From a Local

The video that solves, in practice, the sixty-two minutes between JFK and the hotel: the turnstile, the moment you tap in, the difference between uptown and downtown, and the trap of the express train that runs straight past your station. It is tonight's vocabulary, all of it, shown on the screen and spoken at real speed.

Tip: watch it BEFORE you fly in September — and then a second time, the night before. While you watch, say out loud, for every rule that comes up: "I have to..." or "I don't have to..." or "I can't...". Speaking out loud on your own feels silly, and it is what turns the structure into something automatic.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 7 — Speaking Up with Confidence -- Hotels, Reservations, and When Things Go Wrong

Podcast — 17 min
Easy English Podcast — How to Sound More Polite in English

This is the whole of Lesson 7, in one calm episode: how could, would and the softening words turn an order into a favor. You will hear the exact frames you practiced today — "Could you...?", "Would you mind...?", "I was wondering if..." — and why the polite version is the one that works.

Tip (do this in the car, on the way there): listen once, all the way through, with no pauses. Then write down every request you hear that starts with could, would or do you know — and bring two you could not have made on your own. They become the mini listening lesson in Lesson 8.

Listen on Spotify ↗
YouTube — grammar lesson
How to Make Polite Indirect Questions in English

The one piece of grammar from tonight that Brazilians get wrong: the word order inside an indirect question. This lesson drills exactly that — "Do you know where the gym is?", never "where is the gym" — and shows how the indirect frame makes any question softer with a stranger.

Tip: after each example, pause and say your own version out loud, about your real trip — "Do you know if the pool is open?", "Could you tell me what time breakfast starts?" Say five before you finish the video.

Watch on YouTube ↗
YouTube — conversation (you can listen without watching)
Hotel Front Desk Conversations — English Speaking Practice

Real front-desk exchanges, at natural speed: checking in, asking about amenities, sorting out a problem. It is exactly the scene you will live in New York in September — the desk, the room, the small requests — in the mouths of people who are not teaching, which is where you will meet it for real.

Tip: this works as pure audio — listen in the car, without looking at the screen. Every time the guest asks for something, pause and ask yourself: was that an order or a favor? Then say the polite version out loud, even if theirs was already polite.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 8 — Eating Out with Confidence -- Restaurants, Orders, and Recommendations

YouTube — tonight's words, in the wild
How to Order Food at a Restaurant in English

A step-by-step walk through a real ordering scene: the greeting, the appetizer, the entree, and the moment the server asks "what can I get you?" You will hear the exact openers from tonight — "I'll go with...", "could I get...", "what do you recommend?" — at natural American speed.

Tip: watch it once with English subtitles. The second time, close your eyes and only listen — and every time you hear a recommendation ("I'd recommend...", "you might want to..."), say it out loud after them. Bring the three phrases you think you will use most in September.

Watch on YouTube ↗
Listening lesson — a full ordering dialogue (B1-B2)
Oxford Online English — Ordering in a Restaurant

A complete, natural dialogue between two customers and a waiter — ordering appetizers, entrees, side dishes and drinks — with the audio you can replay as many times as you like. It is exactly the situation you will be in with your supplier in September, and it targets the gap you named yourself: understanding real speech.

Tip (the exercise that pays the most): listen ONCE without reading, and just catch what you can. Then do the four exercises on the page. Finally, play it a third time in the car and repeat every question the customers ask the waiter, out loud, at the same speed.

Open the lesson ↗
Podcast — for the car (the drive to Guarulhos and back)
American English Podcast — Tipping Culture in the U.S.

The one thing that is not on any menu and traps every foreigner: the tip. This episode explains American tipping etiquette in clear, natural English — who you tip, how much (eighteen to twenty percent), and why the tip is never printed on the tab. It is the exact cultural rule you need for the dinner you will host in September.

Tip: listen ONCE all the way through, without pausing, and do not worry about missing words. The second time, pause every 3 or 4 sentences and say out loud, in one sentence, the rule you just heard. Bring 2 numbers or rules that surprised you.

Listen ↗

Lesson 9 — Shopping, Returning, and Negotiating Price -- Being Direct and Polite in American Stores

YouTube — grammar lesson
USED TO / USE TO / BE USED TO — English with Lucy

Tonight's grammar, in one clear lesson. It nails the two traps Brazilians fall into: keeping the -d in "used to" for a past habit, and dropping it after did — "Did you use to...?", never "did you used to". It also shows how "used to" is different from "be used to" (being accustomed to something), so you don't mix them up.

Tip: after each example, pause and say your own version out loud about your real shopping life — "I used to..., but now I...". Say five before the video ends.

Watch on YouTube ↗
YouTube — conversation (you can listen without watching)
English Conversation: Returning an Item at the Store

The exact scene from tonight's role-play, at natural speed: a customer returns an item at the counter, asks about the return policy, and works out a refund or an exchange. It is the real version of the dialogue you practiced with Nathan — in the mouths of people who are not teaching, which is where you will meet it in New York.

Tip: this works as pure audio — listen in the car, without the screen. Every time the customer asks for something, pause and say your own version out loud, even if theirs was already good.

Watch on YouTube ↗
YouTube — shopping vocabulary
English Shopping Vocabulary for Returns, Refunds, and Exchanges

A calm review of the words from tonight, with real examples: refund, exchange, receipt, defective, and the phrases you use at the counter. It reinforces the vocabulary you met today and adds a few more you will hear in an American store, so the words feel familiar before you need them.

Tip: keep a note open and write down every phrase you could actually use in New York. Bring the two best ones to Lesson 10 — they become the warm-up.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 10 — Talking About Your Work -- Explaining Your Role and Industry with Confidence

TED Talk — how to be understood
Julian Treasure — How to Speak So That People Want to Listen

This is the whole point of tonight's lesson in ten minutes: how to say what you mean so that people actually listen. It is not about big words — it is about being clear, warm, and confident. Exactly the skill you need at the Lyon fair or a dinner in New York, when someone asks what your company does.

Tip: watch it once with English subtitles. The second time, close your eyes and only listen. Then write, in one sentence, the one habit you want to steal for your own 90-second company pitch.

Watch on YouTube ↗
Listening — office and business English (B1)
BBC Learning English — English at Work: The Interview

A short, funny audio drama set in a real office, with the transcript on the page so you can check what you catch. The characters talk about work, roles, and what a company does — the exact register you will use when you explain your own job in English. It targets the gap you named yourself: understanding natural speech.

Tip (the one that pays the most): listen ONCE without reading, and just catch what you can. Then read the transcript and listen again. Finally, play it a third time in the car and repeat, out loud, any sentence that describes what someone does at work.

Open the episode ↗
Lesson — talking about your work in English
Oxford Online English — English Job Interview

A complete video and listening lesson on how to talk about your role, your experience, and what your company does — clearly and confidently, in professional English. It gives you ready-made sentences for exactly the situation you will face at the Lyon fair and in meetings with your Chinese partners.

Tip: watch the video once, then do the exercises on the page. Bring three sentences you could use to describe your own role and industry — and try to build at least one of them in the passive voice ("Our products are…").

Open the lesson ↗

Lesson 11 — Navigating Airports and Flights -- Check-in to Boarding (Consolidation)

YouTube — grammar review
The First Conditional — BBC Learning English (Georgie)

Tonight's grammar, reviewed in one clear lesson from a trusted teacher. It nails the shape you practiced: if + present simple, will in the result — and the one trap Brazilians fall into, putting will in the if part. Watch it as a quick refresher, not a first lesson: you already know this.

Tip: after each example, pause and build your own about the airport — "If the flight is delayed, I'll...", "If the gate changes, I'll...". Say five before the video ends.

Watch on YouTube ↗
YouTube — conversation (American English)
Checking In at an Airport — American English (Rachel's English)

The exact scene from tonight's role-play, in clear American English: a traveler checks in, asks about a seat, and handles the bag at the desk. Rachel also shows how the words really sound when a native says them fast — the part you find hardest — so the check-in in New York feels familiar before you get there.

Tip: this works as pure audio — listen in the car on the way to Guarulhos. Every time the traveler asks for something, pause and say your own version out loud.

Watch on YouTube ↗
YouTube — airport announcements (B1/B2)
Airport and Airplane Announcements — Listening Practice

Real airport and airplane announcements at natural speed, with questions — exactly the listening you did tonight, and the skill you told us is hardest. It practices catching the gate number, the delay, the boarding group, and the final call, so a gate change in New York or Paris does not catch you off guard.

Tip: listen once without the text and note what you catch, then listen again and check. Bring the two phrases you missed most to Lesson 12 — they become the warm-up.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 12 — Hotels and Accommodation -- Checking In and Making Requests Like a Pro

Video — hotel check-in in English
Cloud English — How to Check In at a Hotel in English

A clear, slow travel-English lesson that walks through a real hotel check-in, step by step: giving your name, sorting out the reservation, getting your key card, and asking about the room. It is the exact scene you will live in New York in September — hear a native speaker do it first.

Tip: watch it once without stopping. The second time, pause after every question and repeat it out loud — especially any polite request that starts with "Could you" or "I was wondering".

Watch on YouTube ↗
Grammar — polite indirect questions
Arnel's Everyday English — Indirect (Embedded) Questions

This is tonight's grammar, explained slowly and clearly by a teacher whose accent is easy to follow. She shows exactly how the soft opener works and why the word order changes after it — "where the elevator is", not "where is the elevator". Watch it before your lesson and the whole thing will click.

Tip: keep a pen. Every time she gives a direct question, pause and try to turn it into an indirect one yourself before she does. Then check whether you kept the normal word order.

Watch on YouTube ↗
Lesson — hotel English, start to finish
Oxford Online English — Hotel English (Video)

A complete video lesson with dialogues and a quiz, covering everything you need at a hotel: the reservation, checking in, asking about facilities, handling problems, and checking out. It gives you ready-made phrases for exactly the situations in tonight's lesson, plus a vocabulary quiz to test yourself.

Tip: watch the video, then do the quiz on the page. Write down three requests you could make at your own hotel in New York, and turn each one into a polite indirect question.

Open the lesson ↗

Lesson 13 — Asking for Directions -- Finding Your Way Around the City on Foot

YouTube -- grammar review
Prepositions of Place and Movement -- English Grammar (Learn Easy English)

Tonight's grammar in one clear lesson: where things are (across from, next to, opposite, between) and how you move (along, past, through, toward). It shows exactly the difference you practiced -- a place preposition tells you WHERE, a movement preposition tells you HOW to get there.

Tip: after each example, pause and build your own about your walk to the museum -- "It is across from the park", "Walk along Fifth, past the church". Say five before the video ends.

Watch on YouTube ↗
YouTube -- conversation
Asking for and Giving Directions in English (Nmodel)

The exact scene from tonight's role-play: someone stops a stranger, asks the way, and follows the answer, with the phrases and prepositions on screen. It is the same language you practiced -- "Could you tell me how to get to...?", "go along", "turn at the corner", "it is across from..." -- so asking the way in New York feels familiar before you get there.

Tip: this works as pure audio -- listen in the car on the way to Guarulhos. Every time someone asks for directions, pause and say your own version out loud.

Watch on YouTube ↗
YouTube -- directions dialogues (B1)
Asking for Directions -- Everyday English Dialogues (Ellii)

Short, natural directions dialogues at everyday speed -- exactly the listening you did tonight, and the skill you told us is hardest. It practices catching the turn (left or right), the street name, and the landmark, so a real "how do I get to...?" in New York does not catch you off guard.

Tip: listen once without reading and note the direction and the landmark you catch, then listen again and check. Bring the two prepositions you missed most to Lesson 14 -- they become the warm-up.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 14 — Getting Around the City

Video
"HOW to use the NYC Subway (Everything YOU NEED to know)!" -- Sarah Funk

A clear, real-life guide to getting around New York by subway: reading the map, the express vs the local, and the words a traveler actually needs. Natural American English at a normal pace -- perfect listening practice. Connection to Lesson 14: choosing the fastest, most reliable way across the city.

Tip: after watching, compare two options out loud ("the express is quicker than the local") and name your top pick with a superlative.

Watch on YouTube ↗
Podcast
"Keeping cars out of city centres" -- 6 Minute English (BBC)

A short, clear conversation about how cities move people around and why some are limiting traffic. Rich vocabulary about transport, congestion, and getting around -- exactly this lesson's theme, spoken by two clear voices with subtitles. Connection to Lesson 14: comparing transport options in a real city.

Tip: write down three transport words you hear and use each one in a sentence that compares two options.

Listen on YouTube ↗
Grammar Video
"How to use comparatives and superlatives" -- 6 Minute Grammar (BBC)

A focused lesson on the exact grammar of this class: how to form and use comparatives (-er / more + than) and superlatives (the -est / the most), including the irregular forms. Connection to Lesson 14: comparing your options -- "faster than", "the most scenic".

Tip: after each example, make your own sentence comparing two ways to get around your city.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 15 — Small Talk That Works -- Starting, Sustaining, and Exiting Casual Conversations

YouTube -- grammar review
Relative Clauses: Who / Which / Where / That (Teachers Mark and Matt)

Tonight's grammar in one clear lesson: who for people, which and that for things and companies, and where for places. It shows exactly the choice you practiced -- the same little word that connects two ideas into one smooth sentence.

Tip: after each example, pause and build your own about your work -- "I run a company that makes ingredients", "That's the colleague who helped me", "Lyon is a city where...". Say five before the video ends.

Watch on YouTube ↗
YouTube -- conversation
Master Networking at Your First Business Event (Toomey Business English)

The exact scene from tonight's role-play: walking into a business event, opening with a stranger, finding common ground, and exchanging cards. It is the same language you practiced -- "What brings you here?", "I work for a company that...", "let's stay in touch" -- so the reception in Lyon feels familiar before you get there.

Tip: every time a speaker opens or exits a conversation, pause and say your own version out loud, with a relative pronoun about your company or your city.

Watch on YouTube ↗
YouTube -- small talk phrases (B1)
Small Talk: Useful Phrases & Questions for Everyday Conversation (mmmEnglish)

Natural small talk phrases and icebreakers at everyday speed -- exactly the listening you did tonight, and the skill you told us is hardest. It practices catching the opener, the follow-up question, and the graceful exit, so a real "What brings you here?" in Lyon does not catch you off guard.

Tip: this works as pure audio -- listen in the car on the way to Guarulhos. Every time you hear an icebreaker or a follow-up question, pause and say your own out loud. Bring the two phrases you liked most to Lesson 16.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 16 — Getting Around Like a Local

Video
"The 10 Most Walkable Cities in the USA" -- Globe Trekking

A clear tour of the American cities where you can leave the car behind and move on foot, by subway, or by bike. You will hear the exact words from this lesson -- walkable, grid system, urban sprawl -- used in real context, spoken in natural American English. Connection to Lesson 16: how different US cities get you around.

Tip: after watching, report what the narrator said about one city, using "He said that..." and a past-tense verb.

Watch on YouTube ↗
Talk
"In Search of Car-Free America: Why Walkable Places Are Popular" -- CityNerd

A relaxed, clear talk about why some American places are easy to explore on foot while others are built around the highway and urban sprawl. Rich, real vocabulary about getting around -- exactly this lesson's theme -- spoken slowly enough to follow. Connection to Lesson 16: the same New York versus Los Angeles contrast from the reading.

Tip: note three things the speaker says, then report each one to a friend with "He told me that..." and a back-shifted verb.

Listen on YouTube ↗
Grammar Video
"How to use reported speech" -- 6 Minute Grammar (BBC)

A focused lesson on the exact grammar of this class: how to report what someone said, moving the verb one step into the past and choosing between "say" and "tell". Connection to Lesson 16: "She said that the city was walkable", "He told me that I would need a car".

Tip: after each example, report one sentence a friend really said to you this week, back-shifting the verb.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 17 — Checking In, Speaking Up -- Hotels, Reservations, and Handling Problems Confidently

YouTube -- grammar review
May, Can, Could -- Making Polite Requests in English (English Like A Native)

Tonight's grammar in one clear lesson: how could and would soften a request, and why "Could you...?" sounds warmer and more professional than "Can you...?". It is exactly the choice you practiced at the front desk -- firm about the problem, kind to the person.

Tip: after each example, pause and build your own about a hotel -- "Could you upgrade my room?", "Would it be possible to check in early?", "You should speak to the manager". Say five before the video ends.

Watch on YouTube ↗
YouTube -- travel English
How to Check In at a Hotel in English (Cloud English)

The exact scene from tonight's role-play: arriving at the front desk, giving your name, confirming your room type, and asking about checkout. It is the same language you practiced -- "I have a reservation under...", "Could you...?", "Would it be possible to...?" -- so a real check-in in New York feels familiar before you get there.

Tip: every time the guest speaks, pause and say your own version out loud, with a polite modal. Then imagine one problem -- a wrong room type -- and add "There seems to be a discrepancy."

Watch on YouTube ↗
YouTube -- listening & speaking (B1)
At the Hotel -- Making a Complaint (English Speaking Course)

A guest reports a problem at the front desk and asks, politely, for it to be fixed -- exactly the skill you told us is hardest to catch by ear. It practices naming the problem, requesting the fix, and accepting the solution, so a real "There seems to be a problem" in New York does not catch you off guard.

Tip: this works as pure audio -- listen in the car on the way to Guarulhos. Every time the guest makes a polite request, pause and say your own out loud. Bring the two phrases you liked most to Lesson 18.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 18 — Ordering with Confidence

Video
"How to Order Food in a U.S. Restaurant" -- Single Step English

A real-world walkthrough of ordering in an American restaurant: greeting the server, asking about the daily specials, requesting changes, and handling the check. You will hear this lesson's words -- server, special, check -- used in natural context and at natural speed. Connection to Lesson 18: the whole flow of eating out with confidence.

Tip: pause after each phrase and repeat it aloud. Then add one sentence of your own with "If I were you, I would...".

Watch on YouTube ↗
Talk
"How Much to Tip in the US -- A Guide for English Learners" -- Mr. Ian Teaches English

A clear explainer, made for learners, on how tipping, splitting the check, and paying work in the United States -- the exact money culture from today's reading. Spoken slowly enough to follow, with useful restaurant vocabulary throughout. Connection to Lesson 18: why a twenty percent tip is expected and how friends split the check.

Tip: note three facts the speaker mentions, then say each as a hypothetical: "If I ate out there, I would leave twenty percent."

Watch on YouTube ↗
Grammar Video
"The Second Conditional" -- BBC Learning English (6 Minute Grammar)

A focused six-minute lesson on the exact grammar of today's class: how to build the second conditional for imaginary situations, why we use "were" for every subject, and how it differs from the first conditional. Connection to Lesson 18: "If I were you, I would order the house specialty."

Tip: after each example, make one restaurant sentence of your own: "If I had more time, I would try every dish."

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 19 — Small Talk That Works

Lesson 19 — Small Talk That Works -- Modal & Conditional Consolidation

Series
Friends — casual conversation between characters

A classic sitcom built on everyday small talk: openers, reactions, and friendly closings. Perfect for hearing how native speakers keep a light conversation going.

Tip: pick one scene and write down two openers and two reactions you could really use.

Watch on YouTube ↗
Podcast
All Ears English — Start the Conversation / Small Talk

Lindsay and Michelle teach real American small talk: safe topics, natural reactions, and how to keep a casual chat flowing. Listen on your commute to Guarulhos.

Tip: listen to one episode and note one thing that surprised you about how natives talk casually.

Listen ↗
YouTube
Speak Confident English — Small Talk FTW! (24 Phrases to Use)

Annemarie gives 24 ready-to-use small talk phrases to open, react, and keep a conversation going — ideal before your trip to New York.

Tip: after watching, write three phrases you would really use, and record a 2-minute small talk of your own.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 20 — Understanding Real English

Video
"How to Pronounce 'gonna' and 'gotta': Reduction and Linking in American English" -- Rachel's English

A clear, slow-motion breakdown of the exact reductions you heard in class today: how "going to" becomes "gonna" and "got to" becomes "gotta" in fast connected speech. Rachel shows the mouth, the sound, and the rhythm, so your ear learns to expect them. Connection to Lesson 20: the reductions that used to sound like a wall of noise.

Tip: pause after each example, repeat it aloud, then say the full form. Notice how much shorter the reduction feels.

Watch on YouTube ↗
Listening
"Why English Native Speakers Sound So Fast | Connected Speech Explained" -- Pronunciation with Emma

An eye-opening lesson on why native speakers seem to talk so quickly, and why it is really connected speech, not real speed. Emma decodes how words link and blend, so you learn to tune in to the keywords instead of chasing every sound. Connection to Lesson 20: getting the gist of fast English in stores and markets.

Tip: choose three examples from the video and write both what you hear and the full form. Then say each one out loud.

Watch on YouTube ↗
Grammar Video
"Used to - 6 Minute Grammar" -- BBC Learning English

A focused lesson on the exact grammar of today's class: how to use "used to + base verb" to talk about past habits and states that are no longer true, and how the affirmative, negative, and question forms work. Connection to Lesson 20: "I used to freeze when people spoke fast, but now I listen for keywords."

Tip: after the video, record three sentences about how your English used to be and how it is now.

Watch on YouTube ↗
Welcome Key Words The Code Hotel Arts Practice Your Turn Wrap-Up
LESSON 1
T
